title: When Consistency Becomes Bias: Interviewer Effects in Semi-Structured Clinical Interviews
summary: Automatic depression detection from doctor-patient conversations has gained momentum thanks to the availability of public corpora and advances in language modeling. However, interpretability remains limited: strong performance is often reported without revealing what drives predictions. We analyze three datasets: ANDROIDS, DAIC-WOZ, E-DAIC and identify a systematic bias from interviewer prompts in semi-structured interviews. Models trained on interviewer turns exploit fixed prompts and positions to distinguish depressed from control subjects, often achieving high classification scores without using participant language. Restricting models to participant utterances distributes decision evidence more broadly and reflects genuine linguistic cues. While semi-structured protocols ensure consistency, including interviewer prompts inflates performance by leveraging script artifacts. Our results highlight a cross-dataset, architecture-agnostic bias and emphasize the need for analyses that localize decision evidence by time and speaker to ensure models learn from participants' language.

AI.tldr: The paper exposes a systematic bias in automatic depression detection systems: models can achieve high accuracy by exploiting fixed interviewer prompts rather than patients' language.
AI.motivation: To understand what actually drives predictions in automatic depression detection from doctor-patient conversations and to assess whether strong reported performance truly reflects learning from participants’ linguistic cues or from artifacts in semi-structured interview protocols.
AI.method: The authors analyze three depression detection datasets (ANDROIDS, DAIC-WOZ, E-DAIC), train models under different input conditions (including or excluding interviewer turns), and examine where decision evidence is localized over time and by speaker role to diagnose biases.
AI.result: They find a strong, architecture-agnostic, cross-dataset bias: models readily use the fixed interviewer prompts and their positions within semi-structured interviews to distinguish depressed from control participants, achieving high scores even when participant language is not used.
AI.conclusion: Including interviewer prompts in training/testing inflates depression detection performance by exploiting script artifacts instead of genuine patient cues; restricting models to participant utterances yields more meaningful evidence distribution. Future work must analyze decision evidence by time and speaker to ensure models truly learn from participants’ language rather than protocol biases.

title: Demystifying When Pruning Works via Representation Hierarchies
summary: Network pruning, which removes less important parameters or architectures, is often expected to improve efficiency while preserving performance. However, this expectation does not consistently hold across language tasks: pruned models can perform well on non-generative tasks but frequently fail in generative settings. To understand this discrepancy, we analyze network pruning from a representation-hierarchy perspective, decomposing the internal computation of language models into three sequential spaces: embedding (hidden representations), logit (pre-softmax outputs), and probability (post-softmax distributions). We find that representations in the embedding and logit spaces are largely robust to pruning-induced perturbations. However, the nonlinear transformation from logits to probabilities amplifies these deviations, which accumulate across time steps and lead to substantial degradation during generation. In contrast, the stability of the categorical-token probability subspace, together with the robustness of the embedding space, supports the effectiveness of pruning for non-generative tasks such as retrieval and multiple-choice selection. Our analysis disentangles the effects of pruning across tasks and provides practical guidance for its application. Code is available at https://github.com/CASE-Lab-UMD/Pruning-on-Representations

AI.tldr: The paper studies why network pruning behaves differently on generative vs non-generative language tasks, showing that pruning-induced changes are small in hidden and logit spaces but get amplified after softmax during generation, while remaining stable for categorical decisions.
AI.motivation: Although pruning is widely used to compress language models, practitioners observe that pruned models often retain accuracy on classification or retrieval tasks but degrade substantially in text generation. This inconsistency is not well understood. The authors aim to provide a principled explanation by analyzing how pruning perturbs internal representations at different stages of computation, and why these perturbations hurt generative tasks more than non-generative ones.
AI.method: The authors decompose the computation of language models into three spaces: (1) embedding space (hidden representations), (2) logit space (pre-softmax outputs), and (3) probability space (post-softmax token distributions). They then apply network pruning and analyze how it perturbs representations in each space, especially focusing on how small logit changes can be amplified by the softmax nonlinearity. They also look at the accumulation of these perturbations across generation time steps and contrast this with tasks that only rely on a single-step categorical decision, like retrieval or multiple-choice selection.
AI.result: They find that embedding and logit representations remain largely robust to pruning: perturbations are small and often do not significantly harm performance if one looks only at these spaces. However, due to the nonlinear nature of softmax, these small logit perturbations can lead to large changes in probability distributions, especially in generative settings where errors compound autoregressively over multiple time steps. Conversely, for non-generative tasks relying on categorical token probabilities (e.g., picking the argmax token once), the probability subspace remains stable enough to maintain performance after pruning.
AI.conclusion: The discrepancy between pruning performance on generative vs non-generative language tasks arises from how pruning-induced perturbations are transformed and accumulated across representation spaces, particularly by softmax and autoregressive generation. Pruning is more reliable for tasks involving single-step categorical decisions (classification, retrieval, multiple-choice) than for open-ended generation. The paper provides guidance for when and how to apply pruning depending on task type, and releases code to support further study.

title: Fine-Tuning A Large Language Model for Systematic Review Screening
summary: Systematic reviews traditionally have taken considerable amounts of human time and energy to complete, in part due to the extensive number of titles and abstracts that must be reviewed for potential inclusion. Recently, researchers have begun to explore how to use large language models (LLMs) to make this process more efficient. However, research to date has shown inconsistent results. We posit this is because prompting alone may not provide sufficient context for the model(s) to perform well. In this study, we fine-tune a small 1.2 billion parameter open-weight LLM specifically for study screening in the context of a systematic review in which humans rated more than 8500 titles and abstracts for potential inclusion. Our results showed strong performance improvements from the fine-tuned model, with the weighted F1 score improving 80.79% compared to the base model. When run on the full dataset of 8,277 studies, the fine-tuned model had 86.40% agreement with the human coder, a 91.18% true positive rate, a 86.38% true negative rate, and perfect agreement across multiple inference runs. Taken together, our results show that there is promise for fine-tuning LLMs for title and abstract screening in large-scale systematic reviews.

AI.tldr: The paper fine-tunes a small open-weight LLM to screen titles/abstracts for systematic reviews, achieving large gains over the base model and high agreement with human coders, suggesting fine-tuning is a promising way to accelerate systematic review screening.
AI.motivation: Systematic reviews require humans to screen thousands of titles and abstracts, which is time-consuming and labor-intensive. Existing attempts to use LLMs with prompting alone have yielded inconsistent performance, likely because the models lack domain- and task-specific context. The authors aim to see whether task-specific fine-tuning of a compact, open-weight LLM can make title/abstract screening substantially more accurate and reliable, thereby reducing human effort in large-scale systematic reviews.
AI.method: The authors took a 1.2B-parameter open-weight LLM and fine-tuned it for the binary classification task of deciding whether each title/abstract in a systematic review should be included or excluded. Training data consisted of more than 8,500 titles and abstracts that had been manually coded by humans for inclusion decisions. They then evaluated the fine-tuned model against the base model using metrics such as weighted F1, agreement with human coders, true positive rate, true negative rate, and consistency across multiple inference runs.
AI.result: Fine-tuning led to a large performance gain: the weighted F1 score improved by 80.79% compared to the base model. On the full dataset of 8,277 studies, the fine-tuned model achieved 86.40% agreement with the human coder, a 91.18% true positive rate (sensitivity), an 86.38% true negative rate (specificity), and produced identical predictions across repeated inference runs, indicating deterministic or highly stable behavior.
AI.conclusion: Fine-tuning a relatively small open-weight LLM on human-labeled screening decisions can substantially improve performance for title and abstract screening in systematic reviews, achieving high agreement with human coders and robust behavior. This suggests that fine-tuned LLMs are a promising tool to support or partially automate the screening stage of large-scale systematic reviews, potentially saving significant human time and effort compared with traditional manual processes or prompt-only LLM approaches.

title: Evaluating Fine-Tuned LLM Model For Medical Transcription With Small Low-Resource Languages Validated Dataset
summary: Clinical documentation is a critical factor for patient safety, diagnosis, and continuity of care. The administrative burden of EHRs is a significant factor in physician burnout. This is a critical issue for low-resource languages, including Finnish. This study aims to investigate the effectiveness of a domain-aligned natural language processing (NLP); large language model for medical transcription in Finnish by fine-tuning LLaMA 3.1-8B on a small validated corpus of simulated clinical conversations by students at Metropolia University of Applied Sciences. The fine-tuning process for medical transcription used a controlled preprocessing and optimization approach. The fine-tuning effectiveness was evaluated by sevenfold cross-validation. The evaluation metrics for fine-tuned LLaMA 3.1-8B were BLEU = 0.1214, ROUGE-L = 0.4982, and BERTScore F1 = 0.8230. The results showed a low n-gram overlap but a strong semantic similarity with reference transcripts. This study indicate that fine-tuning can be an effective approach for translation of medical discourse in spoken Finnish and support the feasibility of fine-tuning a privacy-oriented domain-specific large language model for clinical documentation in Finnish. Beside that provide directions for future work.

AI.tldr: The paper fine-tunes a LLaMA 3.1-8B model on a small Finnish clinical conversation corpus for medical transcription and shows high semantic similarity to reference transcripts despite low n-gram overlap, indicating feasibility of a privacy-preserving, domain-specific LLM for Finnish clinical documentation.
AI.motivation: Clinical documentation is essential for patient safety, diagnosis, and continuity of care, but EHR-related documentation burden contributes to physician burnout. This issue is especially acute for low-resource languages like Finnish, where tools for automatic medical transcription are scarce. The authors are motivated to explore whether a domain-aligned large language model can reduce documentation workload by accurately transcribing spoken Finnish medical discourse while respecting privacy and working with limited data.
AI.method: The authors fine-tune the LLaMA 3.1-8B large language model on a small, validated corpus of simulated Finnish clinical conversations produced by students at Metropolia University of Applied Sciences. They apply controlled preprocessing and optimization during fine-tuning and evaluate the model’s transcription performance using sevenfold cross-validation. Standard text similarity metrics (BLEU, ROUGE-L, and BERTScore F1) are computed to assess the quality of generated transcripts against reference transcripts.
AI.result: The fine-tuned LLaMA 3.1-8B model achieves BLEU = 0.1214, ROUGE-L = 0.4982, and BERTScore F1 = 0.8230 on the medical transcription task. While the n-gram overlap (BLEU, partly ROUGE-L) is relatively low, the high BERTScore F1 indicates strong semantic similarity between model-generated and reference transcripts, suggesting that the model captures the meaning of clinical discourse even when wording differs.
AI.conclusion: Fine-tuning a large language model like LLaMA 3.1-8B on a small, domain-specific Finnish clinical conversation corpus is a promising strategy for medical transcription in a low-resource language. The resulting model shows strong semantic fidelity to reference transcripts, implying that domain-aligned, privacy-oriented LLMs can support clinical documentation workflows in Finnish. The study also highlights avenues for future work, such as expanding data, improving evaluation, and integrating the model into real-world clinical settings.

title: ARC-AGI-3: A New Challenge for Frontier Agentic Intelligence
summary: We introduce ARC-AGI-3, an interactive benchmark for studying agentic intelligence through novel, abstract, turn-based environments in which agents must explore, infer goals, build internal models of environment dynamics, and plan effective action sequences without explicit instructions. Like its predecessors ARC-AGI-1 and 2, ARC-AGI-3 focuses entirely on evaluating fluid adaptive efficiency on novel tasks, while avoiding language and external knowledge. ARC-AGI-3 environments only leverage Core Knowledge priors and are difficulty-calibrated via extensive testing with human test-takers. Our testing shows humans can solve 100% of the environments, in contrast to frontier AI systems which, as of March 2026, score below 1%. In this paper, we present the benchmark design, its efficiency-based scoring framework grounded in human action baselines, and the methodology used to construct, validate, and calibrate the environments.

AI.tldr: ARC-AGI-3 is a new interactive benchmark to evaluate agentic intelligence in abstract, turn-based environments, emphasizing adaptive problem-solving without language or external knowledge, where humans perform perfectly but current AI systems perform extremely poorly.
AI.motivation: To better study and measure agentic intelligence—agents' ability to explore, infer goals, model dynamics, and plan in novel tasks—using a benchmark that avoids confounds from language and prior world knowledge, and that is tightly calibrated to human capabilities.
AI.method: Design a suite of novel, abstract, turn-based environments that only rely on Core Knowledge priors, define an efficiency-based scoring framework grounded in human action baselines, and use extensive human testing to construct, validate, and difficulty-calibrate the tasks. Compare human performance with frontier AI systems.
AI.result: Humans can solve 100% of the ARC-AGI-3 environments under the benchmark conditions, whereas state-of-the-art AI systems as of March 2026 achieve below 1% performance, demonstrating a large gap between human and artificial agentic intelligence on these tasks.
AI.conclusion: ARC-AGI-3 provides a stringent, language-free benchmark for evaluating adaptive, agentic intelligence, revealing a stark performance gap between humans and current AI systems and offering a grounded framework for future research on agents that can efficiently handle novel abstract tasks.

title: Enhancing Structured Meaning Representations with Aspect Classification
summary: To fully capture the meaning of a sentence, semantic representations should encode aspect, which describes the internal temporal structure of events. In graph-based meaning representation frameworks such as Uniform Meaning Representations (UMR), aspect lets one know how events unfold over time, including distinctions such as states, activities, and completed events. Despite its importance, aspect remains sparsely annotated across semantic meaning representation frameworks. This has, in turn, hindered not only current manual annotation, but also the development of automatic systems capable of predicting aspectual information. In this paper, we introduce a new dataset of English sentences annotated with UMR aspect labels over Abstract Meaning Representation (AMR) graphs that lack the feature. We describe the annotation scheme and guidelines used to label eventive predicates according to the UMR aspect lattice, as well as the annotation pipeline used to ensure consistency and quality across annotators through a multi-step adjudication process. To demonstrate the utility of our dataset for future automation, we present baseline experiments using three modeling approaches. Our results establish initial benchmarks for automatic UMR aspect prediction and provide a foundation for integrating aspect into semantic meaning representations more broadly.

AI.tldr: The paper augments existing semantic meaning representations by adding aspect annotations to AMR graphs using the UMR framework and establishes benchmarks for automatic aspect prediction.
AI.motivation: Aspect, which encodes the internal temporal structure of events (e.g., states, activities, completed events), is crucial for fully capturing sentence meaning but is sparsely annotated in current semantic meaning representation frameworks. This lack of aspect annotation hinders both manual annotation work and the development of automatic systems that can predict aspectual information, motivating the need for a dedicated, high-quality dataset and clear guidelines.
AI.method: The authors create a new dataset of English sentences where UMR aspect labels are added on top of existing AMR graphs that previously lacked aspect information. They design an annotation scheme and detailed guidelines based on the UMR aspect lattice to label eventive predicates. To ensure annotation consistency and quality, they implement a multi-step adjudication pipeline involving multiple annotators. Additionally, they run baseline experiments for automatic aspect prediction using three different modeling approaches to test the dataset’s suitability for machine learning.
AI.result: They obtain a curated dataset of AMR graphs enriched with UMR aspect labels and show, via baseline experiments with three modeling approaches, that this dataset can support automatic prediction of aspect, yielding initial benchmark scores for the task. These results validate the feasibility of learning aspectual information from the annotated data, though there remains substantial room for improvement over the baselines.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that enriching AMR graphs with UMR aspect annotations is feasible and useful. The resulting dataset, together with clearly specified guidelines and an adjudication pipeline, provides a solid foundation for future work on automatic UMR aspect prediction. The established baselines serve as initial benchmarks and demonstrate how aspect can be more broadly integrated into semantic meaning representation frameworks.

title: When Is Collective Intelligence a Lottery? Multi-Agent Scaling Laws for Memetic Drift in LLMs
summary: Multi-agent systems powered by large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in settings that shape consequential decisions, both directly and indirectly. Yet it remains unclear whether their outcomes reflect collective reasoning, systematic bias, or mere chance. Recent work has sharpened this question with naming games, showing that even when no individual agent favors any label a priori, populations rapidly break symmetry and reach consensus. Here, we reveal the mechanism by introducing a minimal model, Quantized Simplex Gossip (QSG), and trace the microscopic origin of this agreement to mutual in-context learning. In QSG, agents maintain internal belief states but learn from one another's sampled outputs, so one agent's arbitrary choice becomes the next agent's evidence and can compound toward agreement. By analogy with neutral evolution, we call this sampling-driven regime memetic drift. QSG predicts a crossover from a drift-dominated regime, where consensus is effectively a lottery, to a selection regime, where weak biases are amplified and shape the outcome. We derive scaling laws for drift-induced polarization as a function of population size, communication bandwidth, in-context adaptation rate, and agents' internal uncertainty, and we validate them in both QSG simulations and naming-game experiments with LLM populations. Together, these results provide a framework for studying the collective mechanisms of social representation formation in multi-agent systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a minimal model, Quantized Simplex Gossip (QSG), to explain how LLM-based multi-agent systems rapidly reach consensus via mutual in-context learning, revealing a drift-versus-selection mechanism similar to neutral evolution.
AI.motivation: LLM-based multi-agent systems are increasingly used in consequential decision-making, but it is unclear whether their collective outcomes arise from genuine collective reasoning, systematic biases, or random fluctuations. Prior work with naming games shows symmetry breaking and rapid consensus even when no label is favored initially, but the underlying mechanism is not well understood. The authors aim to build a principled, minimal model that explains how such consensus emerges and when it reflects chance versus bias amplification.
AI.method: The authors propose Quantized Simplex Gossip (QSG), a minimal mathematical / computational model where agents maintain internal belief states represented over a simplex and communicate via sampled discrete outputs. Each agent updates its belief through mutual in-context learning from other agents’ samples, creating a feedback loop where random early choices can propagate. They analyze the model theoretically, drawing an analogy to neutral evolution and introducing the concept of memetic drift. They derive scaling laws that relate drift-induced polarization and consensus dynamics to parameters such as population size, communication bandwidth, in-context adaptation rate, and internal uncertainty. They then validate these predictions with QSG simulations and with naming-game experiments using populations of LLM agents.
AI.result: The QSG model explains how mutual in-context learning can turn arbitrary early samples into shared evidence that cascades into population-wide agreement—memetic drift. The analysis shows a crossover between a drift-dominated regime, where consensus outcomes are effectively random (a lottery), and a selection regime, where even weak underlying biases are amplified and systematically determine the consensus. The authors derive scaling laws that quantify how drift-induced polarization depends on population size, communication bandwidth, adaptation rate, and internal uncertainty, and they confirm these laws empirically in both simulations and LLM-based naming-game experiments.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the emergent consensus behaviors observed in LLM-based multi-agent systems can be understood through the mechanism of mutual in-context learning, formalized as memetic drift in the QSG model. It shows that such systems can operate in a drift-dominated regime, where outcomes are largely random, or in a selection regime, where small biases are amplified—clarifying when consensus reflects chance versus structured preference. This framework provides a principled way to study and eventually control how social representations and shared labels form in LLM multi-agent populations, with implications for the design and governance of such systems in practice.

title: Synthetic Rewriting as a Quality Multiplier: Evidence from Portuguese Continued Pretraining
summary: Synthetic data generation through document rewriting has emerged as a promising technique for improving language model pretraining, yet most studies focus on English and do not systematically control for the quality of the source data being rewritten. We present a controlled study of how synthetic rewriting interacts with source data quality in the context of Portuguese continued pretraining. Starting from ClassiCC-PT, a Portuguese corpus annotated with STEM and Educational quality scores, we construct two 10B-token subsets at different quality levels and rewrite each into four styles using a 7B instruction-tuned model, producing approximately 40B tokens of synthetic data per condition. We train two English-centric base models (1.1B and 7B parameters) on each condition and evaluate on PoETa V2, a comprehensive 44-task Portuguese benchmark. At the 7B scale, rewriting high-quality data yields a +3.4 NPM gain over the same data unmodified, while rewriting low-quality data provides only +0.5 NPM. At the 1.1B scale, this interaction is weaker, with unmodified low-quality data performing comparably to rewritten high-quality data. Our results demonstrate that synthetic rewriting acts primarily as a quality multiplier rather than a substitute for data curation, and that this effect is scale-dependent.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how rewriting Portuguese pretraining data with an LLM affects downstream performance, explicitly controlling for source data quality and model scale.
AI.motivation: Most synthetic data rewriting work is done in English and rarely controls for the underlying quality of the original data, leaving unclear whether gains stem from rewriting itself or from better source data. For Portuguese, resources are more limited and understanding how to best leverage synthetic rewriting for continued pretraining is important to build stronger models efficiently. The authors aim to clarify whether rewriting can compensate for low-quality data or mainly amplifies existing quality, and how this interacts with model size.
AI.method: They use ClassiCC-PT, a Portuguese corpus annotated with STEM and Educational quality scores, to build two 10B-token subsets representing high- and low-quality data. Each subset is synthetically rewritten into four textual styles using a 7B instruction-tuned model, yielding about 40B tokens of synthetic data per quality condition. Two English-centric base LLMs (1.1B and 7B parameters) are then continued-pretrained on these various conditions (original vs rewritten, high vs low quality). Performance is evaluated on PoETa V2, a 44-task Portuguese benchmark, using negative log perplexity (NPM) as the main metric.
AI.result: For the 7B model, continuing pretraining on rewritten high-quality data gives a +3.4 NPM improvement compared to training on the same high-quality data without rewriting, whereas rewriting low-quality data yields only a modest +0.5 NPM gain. For the 1.1B model, the interaction between quality and rewriting is weaker: models trained on unmodified low-quality data perform similarly to those trained on rewritten high-quality data, indicating limited leverage from rewriting at small scale.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic rewriting is most effective when applied to already high-quality data and at larger model scales, acting as a quality multiplier rather than a replacement for good data curation. Rewriting cannot fully compensate for poor-quality source data, and its benefits become more pronounced for larger models. Careful selection and curation of source corpora remain crucial, especially when planning large-scale synthetic data generation for non-English languages like Portuguese.

title: AutoSAM: an Agentic Framework for Automating Input File Generation for the SAM Code with Multi-Modal Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: In the design and safety analysis of advanced reactor systems, constructing input files for system-level thermal-hydraulics codes such as the System Analysis Module (SAM) remains a labor-intensive task. Analysts must extract and reconcile design data from heterogeneous engineering documents and manually translate it into solver-specific syntax. In this paper, we present AutoSAM, an agentic framework that automates SAM input file generation. The framework combines a large language model agent with retrieval-augmented generation over the solver's user guide and theory manual, together with specialized tools for analyzing PDFs, images, spreadsheets, and text files. AutoSAM ingests unstructured engineering documents, including system diagrams, design reports, and data tables, extracts simulation-relevant parameters into a human-auditable intermediate representation, and synthesizes validated, solver-compatible input decks. Its multimodal retrieval pipeline integrates scientific text extraction, vision-based figure interpretation, semantic embedding, and query answering. We evaluate AutoSAM on four case studies of increasing complexity: a single-pipe steady-state model, a solid-fuel channel with temperature reactivity feedback, the Advanced Burner Test Reactor core, and the Molten Salt Reactor Experiment primary loop. Across all cases, the agent produces runnable SAM models consistent with expected thermal-hydraulic behavior while explicitly identifying missing data and labeling assumed values. The framework achieves 100% utilization of structured inputs, about 88% extraction from PDF text, and 100% completeness in vision-based geometric extraction. These results demonstrate a practical path toward prompt-driven reactor modeling, in which analysts provide system descriptions and supporting documentation while the agent translates them into transparent, and executable, SAM simulations.

AI.tldr: AutoSAM is an AI-agent framework that automatically generates SAM thermal-hydraulics input files from heterogeneous reactor design documents using multimodal retrieval and a large language model.
AI.motivation: Creating input files for system-level thermal-hydraulics codes like SAM is currently manual, time-consuming, and error-prone because engineers must interpret diverse design documents and map them into solver-specific syntax. This bottleneck slows design iteration and safety analysis for advanced reactors and limits broader accessibility of these tools.
AI.method: The authors develop AutoSAM, an agentic system built around a large language model with retrieval-augmented generation over SAM’s manuals. AutoSAM uses specialized tools for PDF, image, spreadsheet, and text analysis to ingest unstructured engineering artifacts (reports, diagrams, tables). It extracts relevant parameters into a human-auditable intermediate representation, then synthesizes SAM-compatible input decks. The multimodal pipeline combines scientific text parsing, computer vision–based figure understanding, semantic embeddings, and query answering to ground the LLM’s generation. The system is evaluated using four reactor-related case studies of increasing complexity.
AI.result: In four benchmark problems—a simple pipe model, a solid-fuel channel with feedback, the Advanced Burner Test Reactor core, and the Molten Salt Reactor Experiment primary loop—AutoSAM consistently produces runnable SAM models with reasonable thermal-hydraulic behavior. It fully utilizes all available structured inputs, correctly recovers about 88% of relevant information from PDF text, and achieves complete extraction of geometric data from figures. The agent also flags missing data and explicitly annotates assumed values, supporting transparency.
AI.conclusion: AutoSAM shows that prompt-driven reactor modeling is feasible: analysts can submit natural-language system descriptions with accompanying design documents and have an AI agent generate transparent, executable SAM simulations. The framework substantially reduces manual modeling effort while maintaining traceability and auditability, suggesting a pathway to more efficient and accessible advanced reactor design and safety analysis workflows.

title: Prune as You Generate: Online Rollout Pruning for Faster and Better RLVR
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has significantly advanced the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, methods such as GRPO and DAPO suffer from substantial computational cost, since they rely on sampling many rollouts for each prompt. Moreover, in RLVR the relative advantage is often sparse: many samples become nearly all-correct or all-incorrect, yielding low within-group reward variance and thus weak learning signals. In this paper, we introduce arrol (Accelerating RLVR via online Rollout Pruning), an online rollout pruning method that prunes rollouts during generation while explicitly steering the surviving ones more correctness-balanced to enhance learning signals. Specifically, arrol trains a lightweight quality head on-the-fly to predict the success probability of partial rollouts and uses it to make early pruning decisions. The learned quality head can further weigh candidates to improve inference accuracy during test-time scaling. To improve efficiency, we present a system design that prunes rollouts inside the inference engine and re-batches the remaining ones for log-probability computation and policy updates. Across GRPO and DAPO on Qwen-3 and LLaMA-3.2 models (1B-8B), arrol improves average accuracy by +2.30 to +2.99 while achieving up to 1.7x training speedup, and yielding up to +8.33 additional gains in average accuracy in test-time scaling. The code is available at https://github.com/Hsu1023/ARRoL.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ARRoL, an online rollout-pruning method that makes RL with verifiable rewards more sample-efficient and accurate by pruning low-promise rollouts early and steering remaining ones to provide stronger learning signals.
AI.motivation: Existing RLVR methods like GRPO and DAPO greatly improve LLM reasoning but are very computationally expensive because they require many rollouts per prompt. Additionally, the reward structure is often sparse at the group level—most samples for a prompt end up almost all correct or all incorrect—leading to low reward variance and weak gradients. The authors aim to reduce compute while increasing the effectiveness of the learning signal in RLVR training.
AI.method: ARRoL trains a lightweight quality head online during RLVR training to estimate the success probability of partial rollouts as they are generated. Using these predictions, the method prunes low-promise rollouts early and explicitly biases the remaining rollouts toward a more balanced mix of correct and incorrect outcomes, increasing within-group reward variance. System-wise, ARRoL integrates pruning directly into the inference engine and dynamically re-batches the surviving rollouts for log-probability computation and policy updates. The same quality head is reused at test time to reweight candidate generations and improve inference accuracy during test-time scaling.
AI.result: When plugged into GRPO and DAPO on Qwen-3 and LLaMA-3.2 models ranging from 1B to 8B parameters, ARRoL yields 2.30–2.99 average accuracy improvements while providing up to 1.7× speedup in training. At test-time scaling, using the learned quality head to weight candidates further boosts accuracy by up to 8.33 points on average.
AI.conclusion: Online rollout pruning via ARRoL makes RLVR substantially more efficient and effective by focusing compute on promising, learning-signal-rich rollouts. The method not only accelerates training but also improves final model accuracy, and its learned quality head provides additional gains at inference time through better candidate weighting.

title: Trust as Monitoring: Evolutionary Dynamics of User Trust and AI Developer Behaviour
summary: AI safety is an increasingly urgent concern as the capabilities and adoption of AI systems grow. Existing evolutionary models of AI governance have primarily examined incentives for safe development and effective regulation, typically representing users' trust as a one-shot adoption choice rather than as a dynamic, evolving process shaped by repeated interactions. We instead model trust as reduced monitoring in a repeated, asymmetric interaction between users and AI developers, where checking AI behaviour is costly. Using evolutionary game theory, we study how user trust strategies and developer choices between safe (compliant) and unsafe (non-compliant) AI co-evolve under different levels of monitoring cost and institutional regimes. We complement the infinite-population replicator analysis with stochastic finite-population dynamics and reinforcement learning (Q-learning) simulations. Across these approaches, we find three robust long-run regimes: no adoption with unsafe development, unsafe but widely adopted systems, and safe systems that are widely adopted. Only the last is desirable, and it arises when penalties for unsafe behaviour exceed the extra cost of safety and users can still afford to monitor at least occasionally. Our results formally support governance proposals that emphasise transparency, low-cost monitoring, and meaningful sanctions, and they show that neither regulation alone nor blind user trust is sufficient to prevent evolutionary drift towards unsafe or low-adoption outcomes.

AI.tldr: The paper uses evolutionary game theory and simulations to study how user trust and developer safety choices co-evolve, identifying when AI systems end up safe and widely adopted.
AI.motivation: To move beyond static, one-shot models of AI adoption and governance by explicitly modelling how user trust, monitoring behaviour, and developer incentives around safety co-evolve over time in realistic, repeated interactions.
AI.method: They model a repeated asymmetric game between users and AI developers where users can choose costly monitoring and developers can choose safe (compliant) or unsafe (non-compliant) AI. They analyse the model with infinite-population replicator dynamics, stochastic finite-population evolutionary dynamics, and reinforcement learning (Q-learning) simulations under different monitoring costs and institutional penalty regimes.
AI.result: Across all analytical and simulation approaches, they consistently find three long-run regimes: (1) no adoption with unsafe development, (2) unsafe but widely adopted AI systems, and (3) safe and widely adopted systems. The desirable safe-and-adopted regime only emerges when penalties for unsafe behaviour exceed the extra cost of safety and users still monitor at least sometimes.
AI.conclusion: Effective AI safety requires institutional conditions that make unsafe behaviour sufficiently costly while keeping monitoring affordable for users. Transparency, low-cost monitoring, and meaningful sanctions are jointly necessary; regulation without user oversight or blind user trust without penalties leads evolutionarily either to unsafe AI or to low adoption.

title: Formal Semantics for Agentic Tool Protocols: A Process Calculus Approach
summary: The emergence of large language model agents capable of invoking external tools has created urgent need for formal verification of agent protocols. Two paradigms dominate this space: Schema-Guided Dialogue (SGD), a research framework for zero-shot API generalization, and the Model Context Protocol (MCP), an industry standard for agent-tool integration. While both enable dynamic service discovery through schema descriptions, their formal relationship remains unexplored. Building on prior work establishing the conceptual convergence of these paradigms, we present the first process calculus formalization of SGD and MCP, proving they are structurally bisimilar under a well-defined mapping Phi. However, we demonstrate that the reverse mapping Phi^{-1} is partial and lossy, revealing critical gaps in MCP's expressivity. Through bidirectional analysis, we identify five principles -- semantic completeness, explicit action boundaries, failure mode documentation, progressive disclosure compatibility, and inter-tool relationship declaration -- as necessary and sufficient conditions for full behavioral equivalence. We formalize these principles as type-system extensions MCP+, proving MCP+ is isomorphic to SGD. Our work provides the first formal foundation for verified agent systems and establishes schema quality as a provable safety property.

AI.tldr: The paper formally compares two major paradigms for tool-using LLM agents (Schema-Guided Dialogue and Model Context Protocol), proves when they are behaviorally equivalent, shows current MCP is less expressive, and proposes a type-safe extension MCP+ that makes it fully equivalent to SGD and supports formal verification of agent protocols.
AI.motivation: LLM agents increasingly call external tools and services, but there is no solid formal foundation to verify that their interaction protocols are safe, correct, and complete. Two dominant but independently-developed paradigms (SGD from research and MCP from industry) both rely on schemas for dynamic tool discovery, yet their precise formal relationship and relative expressivity have not been clarified. Without such a foundation, it is hard to reason about safety, correctness, and schema quality for real-world agent systems.
AI.method: The authors develop the first process-calculus-based formalization of both Schema-Guided Dialogue (SGD) and Model Context Protocol (MCP). They define a mapping Phi from SGD specifications to MCP specifications and prove structural bisimilarity under this mapping. They then analyze the inverse mapping Phi^{-1}, showing it is partial and lossy, which uncovers expressivity gaps in MCP. From this bidirectional analysis, they distill five design principles and encode them as type-system extensions in a new protocol variant called MCP+. They prove that MCP+ is isomorphic to SGD in the process calculus sense.
AI.result: The paper proves that, when translated via a mapping Phi, SGD and MCP systems exhibit the same observable behavior (structural bisimilarity). However, the reverse translation from MCP back to SGD is not always possible and loses information, formally demonstrating that MCP lacks certain expressive features present in SGD. By introducing five additional principles and capturing them in a typed extension MCP+, the authors restore full expressiveness and prove MCP+ and SGD are isomorphic. They also show that under this framework, properties like schema quality and safety can be represented and verified as type-level properties.
AI.conclusion: SGD and MCP, though developed in different communities, are formally behaviorally equivalent once MCP is extended with a principled type system (MCP+). Current MCP is strictly less expressive than SGD, but the five identified principles provide necessary and sufficient conditions to bridge the gap. With MCP+, schema quality and protocol safety become formally verifiable properties rather than informal best practices. This work lays a foundational theory for verified LLM agent-tool protocols and offers concrete guidance for designing future-safe, verifiable agent integration standards.

title: Estimating near-verbatim extraction risk in language models with decoding-constrained beam search
summary: Recent work shows that standard greedy-decoding extraction methods for quantifying memorization in LLMs miss how extraction risk varies across sequences. Probabilistic extraction -- computing the probability of generating a target suffix given a prefix under a decoding scheme -- addresses this, but is tractable only for verbatim memorization, missing near-verbatim instances that pose similar privacy and copyright risks. Quantifying near-verbatim extraction risk is expensive: the set of near-verbatim suffixes is combinatorially large, and reliable Monte Carlo (MC) estimation can require ~100,000 samples per sequence. To mitigate this cost, we introduce decoding-constrained beam search, which yields deterministic lower bounds on near-verbatim extraction risk at a cost comparable to ~20 MC samples per sequence. Across experiments, our approach surfaces information invisible to verbatim methods: many more extractable sequences, substantially larger per-sequence extraction mass, and patterns in how near-verbatim extraction risk manifests across model sizes and types of text.

AI.tldr: They propose a new efficient method to measure how likely large language models are to leak near-verbatim training data, showing that existing verbatim-only methods miss much of the real extraction risk.
AI.motivation: Existing memorization metrics mostly look at exact verbatim extraction and typically use greedy decoding. This overlooks how extraction risk changes across different continuations and completely misses near-verbatim copies, which still pose serious privacy and copyright risks. Measuring near-verbatim extraction probabilistically is computationally prohibitive because the space of similar suffixes is huge and Monte Carlo estimation needs many samples for reliability. The paper is motivated by the need for a more computationally efficient yet informative way to quantify near-verbatim extraction risk in LLMs.
AI.method: They formalize probabilistic extraction as the probability of generating a target suffix given a prefix under a specified decoding scheme, then extend this from strict verbatim to near-verbatim matches. To handle the combinatorial explosion of near-verbatim suffixes, they introduce decoding-constrained beam search, a deterministic search algorithm that explores only continuations consistent with a near-verbatim constraint. This yields a lower bound on the near-verbatim extraction probability while keeping computation comparable to using around 20 Monte Carlo samples per sequence, rather than ~100,000.
AI.result: Their method efficiently yields deterministic lower bounds on near-verbatim extraction risk for individual sequences. Empirically, compared with verbatim-only or greedy methods, it identifies many more sequences as extractable, reveals much larger extraction probability mass per sequence, and uncovers systematic patterns in how near-verbatim extraction risk scales with model size and varies across different types of text.
AI.conclusion: Near-verbatim extraction risk in LLMs is both more prevalent and more structured than verbatim-only analyses suggest. Decoding-constrained beam search provides a practical, low-cost tool to quantify this risk, showing that standard memorization assessments significantly underestimate how much training data can be reconstructed. This has direct implications for evaluating privacy and copyright risks of deployed language models.

title: Supervising Ralph Wiggum: Exploring a Metacognitive Co-Regulation Agentic AI Loop for Engineering Design
summary: The engineering design research community has studied agentic AI systems that use Large Language Model (LLM) agents to automate the engineering design process. However, these systems are prone to some of the same pathologies that plague humans. Just as human designers, LLM design agents can fixate on existing paradigms and fail to explore alternatives when solving design challenges, potentially leading to suboptimal solutions. In this work, we propose (1) a novel Self-Regulation Loop (SRL), in which the Design Agent self-regulates and explicitly monitors its own metacognition, and (2) a novel Co-Regulation Design Agentic Loop (CRDAL), in which a Metacognitive Co-Regulation Agent assists the Design Agent in metacognition to mitigate design fixation, thereby improving system performance for engineering design tasks. In the battery pack design problem examined here, we found that the novel CRDAL system generates designs with better performance, without significantly increasing the computational cost, compared to a plain Ralph Wiggum Loop (RWL) and the metacognitively self-assessing Self-Regulation Loop (SRL). Also, we found that the CRDAL system navigated through the latent design space more effectively than both SRL and RWL. However, the SRL did not generate designs with significantly better performance than RWL, even though it explored a different region of the design space. The proposed system architectures and findings of this work provide practical implications for future development of agentic AI systems for engineering design.

AI.tldr: They propose and test two metacognitive agent loops for LLM-based engineering design, showing that a co-regulated loop improves design quality and design-space exploration over baselines in a battery pack case study.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agentic systems for engineering design can suffer from design fixation, similar to human designers, causing them to stick to existing paradigms and miss better alternatives. There is a need for architectures that explicitly monitor and regulate the agents’ own thinking processes to reduce fixation and improve design outcomes without excessive computational cost.
AI.method: They design two architectures: (1) a Self-Regulation Loop (SRL), where the Design Agent explicitly monitors and regulates its own metacognition, and (2) a Co-Regulation Design Agentic Loop (CRDAL), where a separate Metacognitive Co-Regulation Agent monitors and guides the Design Agent’s metacognition. They compare these against a baseline Ralph Wiggum Loop (RWL) using an engineering battery pack design problem, evaluating performance metrics and how effectively each system explores the latent design space and the associated computational cost.
AI.result: CRDAL produces battery pack designs with better performance than both RWL and SRL while not significantly increasing computational cost. CRDAL also explores the latent design space more effectively than the other two systems. SRL explores a different region of design space than RWL but does not yield significantly better performance than RWL.
AI.conclusion: Introducing an explicit metacognitive co-regulation agent (CRDAL) can meaningfully improve the performance and design-space exploration of LLM-based engineering design systems, while a purely self-regulating loop (SRL) may change exploration behavior without clear performance gains. These findings suggest that co-regulated metacognition is a promising direction for building more effective agentic AI systems for engineering design tasks.

title: Toward domain-specific machine translation and quality estimation systems
summary: Machine Translation (MT) and Quality Estimation (QE) perform well in general domains but degrade under domain mismatch. This dissertation studies how to adapt MT and QE systems to specialized domains through a set of data-focused contributions. Chapter 2 presents a similarity-based data selection method for MT. Small, targeted in-domain subsets outperform much larger generic datasets and reach strong translation quality at lower computational cost. Chapter 3 introduces a staged QE training pipeline that combines domain adaptation with lightweight data augmentation. The method improves performance across domains, languages, and resource settings, including zero-shot and cross-lingual cases. Chapter 4 studies the role of subword tokenization and vocabulary in fine-tuning. Aligned tokenization-vocabulary setups lead to stable training and better translation quality, while mismatched configurations reduce performance. Chapter 5 proposes a QE-guided in-context learning method for large language models. QE models select examples that improve translation quality without parameter updates and outperform standard retrieval methods. The approach also supports a reference-free setup, reducing reliance on a single reference set. These results show that domain adaptation depends on data selection, representation, and efficient adaptation strategies. The dissertation provides methods for building MT and QE systems that perform reliably in domain-specific settings.

AI.tldr: The dissertation develops data-centric methods to adapt machine translation (MT) and quality estimation (QE) systems to specialized domains, improving robustness and efficiency under domain mismatch.
AI.motivation: MT and QE systems perform well in general domains but their quality drops when applied to specialized domains with different distributions and limited labeled data. There is a need for systematic, data-focused strategies that make MT and QE robust to domain shifts while keeping computational and annotation costs manageable.
AI.method: The work proposes four main techniques: (1) a similarity-based data selection approach that chooses small but highly relevant in-domain subsets for MT training instead of using large generic corpora; (2) a staged QE training pipeline that first adapts to the target domain and then applies lightweight data augmentation to enhance robustness, across languages and resource conditions; (3) an analysis of subword tokenization and vocabulary choices for fine-tuning, comparing aligned vs. mismatched configurations and their impact on stability and performance; and (4) a QE-guided in-context learning framework for large language models, where QE models select example translations for prompts, enabling effective MT improvements without parameter updates and allowing reference-free operation.
AI.result: (1) Carefully selected in-domain data subsets can outperform much larger generic datasets in MT while lowering computational cost. (2) The staged QE pipeline consistently improves QE performance across domains, languages, and even in zero-shot and cross-lingual scenarios. (3) Using aligned tokenization and vocabulary leads to more stable fine-tuning and better translation quality, whereas mismatched setups harm performance. (4) QE-guided in-context learning for LLMs yields better translation quality than standard example retrieval methods and works in a reference-free setting, reducing dependence on fixed reference translations.
AI.conclusion: Domain adaptation for MT and QE is strongly influenced by data selection, representation, and adaptation strategy. By optimizing which data to use, how to represent it (tokenization/vocabulary), and how to adapt models efficiently (including in-context methods), it is possible to build MT and QE systems that generalize reliably to domain-specific scenarios with improved quality and reduced computational and annotation costs.

title: ReLope: KL-Regularized LoRA Probes for Multimodal LLM Routing
summary: Routing has emerged as a promising strategy for balancing performance and cost in large language model (LLM) systems that combine lightweight models with powerful but expensive large models. Recent studies show that \emph{probe routing}, which predicts the correctness of a small model using its hidden states, provides an effective solution in text-only LLMs. However, we observe that these probes degrade substantially when applied to multimodal LLMs (MLLMs). Through empirical analysis, we find that the presence of visual inputs weakens the separability of correctness signals in hidden states, making them harder to extract using standard probe designs. To address this challenge, we introduce two complementary approaches for improving probe routing in MLLMs. First, we propose the \emph{Attention Probe}, which aggregates hidden states from the preceding layer based on attention scores to recover distributed correctness signals. Second, we present the \emph{KL-Regularized LoRA Probe (ReLope)}, which inserts a lightweight LoRA adapter and applies a KL regularizer to learn routing-aware representations. Comprehensive experiments show that our methods consistently outperform baselines, suggesting that improving the quality of hidden states is key to effective routing in MLLMs. Our code is available at https://github.com/Spinozaaa/ReLope.

AI.tldr: They study why existing “probe routing” methods for choosing between small and large models work poorly in multimodal LLMs and propose two new probe designs that restore routing performance.
AI.motivation: Large multimodal LLM systems often combine a cheap small model with an expensive large model and need a router that decides when the small model is sufficient. Probe routing that predicts if the small model is correct from its hidden states works in text-only LLMs but degrades in multimodal settings, likely due to visual inputs disrupting the separability of correctness information in hidden representations. This raises the need for routing methods tailored to multimodal architectures.
AI.method: They empirically analyze hidden states in multimodal LLMs to show that visual inputs weaken the separability of correctness signals for standard probes. To fix this, they propose two probes: (1) Attention Probe, which uses attention scores to aggregate hidden states from the preceding layer so that distributed correctness signals can be recovered; (2) KL-Regularized LoRA Probe (ReLope), which introduces a lightweight LoRA adapter into the model and adds a KL-divergence regularizer to encourage routing-aware representations. They evaluate these approaches on multimodal routing tasks against existing probe-based baselines.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple multimodal settings show that both proposed probes consistently outperform prior probe-routing baselines. The findings indicate that more informative or adapted hidden states significantly improve routing performance in multimodal LLMs.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that standard probe routing methods do not directly transfer from text-only LLMs to multimodal LLMs because visual inputs blur correctness signals in hidden states. By redesigning probes to better aggregate information (Attention Probe) and to learn routing-aware representations (ReLope with KL-regularized LoRA), routing accuracy can be substantially improved. This underscores the importance of improving hidden-state quality for effective and cost-efficient routing in multimodal LLM systems.

title: LLM-Driven Reasoning for Constraint-Aware Feature Selection in Industrial Systems
summary: Feature selection is a crucial step in large-scale industrial machine learning systems, directly affecting model accuracy, efficiency, and maintainability. Traditional feature selection methods rely on labeled data and statistical heuristics, making them difficult to apply in production environments where labeled data are limited and multiple operational constraints must be satisfied. To address this, we propose Model Feature Agent (MoFA), a model-driven framework that performs sequential, reasoning-based feature selection using both semantic and quantitative feature information. MoFA incorporates feature definitions, importance scores, correlations, and metadata (e.g., feature groups or types) into structured prompts and selects features through interpretable, constraint-aware reasoning. We evaluate MoFA in three real-world industrial applications: (1) True Interest and Time-Worthiness Prediction, where it improves accuracy while reducing feature group complexity, (2) Value Model Enhancement, where it discovers high-order interaction terms that yield substantial engagement gains in online experiments, and (3) Notification Behavior Prediction, where it selects compact, high-value feature subsets that improve both model accuracy and inference efficiency. Together, these results demonstrate the practicality and effectiveness of LLM-based reasoning for feature selection in real production systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MoFA, an LLM-based, reasoning-driven framework for industrial feature selection that uses semantic and quantitative information to choose compact, effective feature sets under real-world constraints.
AI.motivation: In large-scale industrial machine learning, feature selection critically impacts model performance, latency, and maintenance. Existing methods depend heavily on labeled data and simple statistical heuristics, which makes them hard to use when labeled data are scarce and when there are complex business and operational constraints (e.g., cost of computing certain features, dependency between feature groups). The authors want a more flexible, interpretable, and constraint-aware way to select features that fits real production environments.
AI.method: The authors propose Model Feature Agent (MoFA), a model-driven, sequential feature selection framework that uses large language models. MoFA encodes diverse information about features—definitions, historical importance scores, correlations, and metadata like groups and types—into structured prompts. The LLM then performs step-by-step, interpretable reasoning to select features while respecting constraints (such as limiting feature groups, ensuring diversity, or controlling complexity). The process is iterative, enabling the system to refine feature choices using feedback from model performance or changed constraints.
AI.result: MoFA is evaluated on three real-world industrial tasks: (1) True Interest and Time-Worthiness Prediction, where it simultaneously improves prediction accuracy and reduces feature group complexity; (2) Value Model Enhancement, where it identifies higher-order interaction features that, when deployed, lead to significant engagement improvements in online A/B tests; and (3) Notification Behavior Prediction, where it finds compact feature subsets that boost both accuracy and inference efficiency. Across these use cases, MoFA outperforms or complements traditional feature selection methods in terms of accuracy, operational cost, and practicality.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that LLM-based, reasoning-centric feature selection is feasible and beneficial in real production systems. MoFA can integrate heterogeneous feature information and complex constraints to produce interpretable, high-value feature sets that improve model performance and efficiency, showing that LLMs can act as effective agents for industrial feature engineering and selection.

title: Approaches to Analysing Historical Newspapers Using LLMs
summary: This study presents a computational analysis of the Slovene historical newspapers \textit{Slovenec} and \textit{Slovenski narod} from the sPeriodika corpus, combining topic modelling, large language model (LLM)-based aspect-level sentiment analysis, entity-graph visualisation, and qualitative discourse analysis to examine how collective identities, political orientations, and national belonging were represented in public discourse at the turn of the twentieth century. Using BERTopic, we identify major thematic patterns and show both shared concerns and clear ideological differences between the two newspapers, reflecting their conservative-Catholic and liberal-progressive orientations. We further evaluate four instruction-following LLMs for targeted sentiment classification in OCR-degraded historical Slovene and select the Slovene-adapted GaMS3-12B-Instruct model as the most suitable for large-scale application, while also documenting important limitations, particularly its stronger performance on neutral sentiment than on positive or negative sentiment. Applied at dataset scale, the model reveals meaningful variation in the portrayal of collective identities, with some groups appearing predominantly in neutral descriptive contexts and others more often in evaluative or conflict-related discourse. We then create NER graphs to explore the relationships between collective identities and places. We apply a mixed methods approach to analyse the named entity graphs, combining quantitative network analysis with critical discourse analysis. The investigation focuses on the emergence and development of intertwined historical political and socionomic identities. Overall, the study demonstrates the value of combining scalable computational methods with critical interpretation to support digital humanities research on noisy historical newspaper data.

AI.tldr: Computational study of two Slovene historical newspapers using topic modelling, LLM-based sentiment, and entity graphs to examine representations of collective identities and political orientations around 1900.
AI.motivation: To understand how collective identities, political orientations, and national belonging were represented in Slovene public discourse at the turn of the twentieth century, using scalable computational methods that can handle noisy, OCR-degraded historical newspaper data.
AI.method: The authors apply BERTopic for topic modelling to detect thematic patterns in two historical newspapers, evaluate four instruction-following LLMs for aspect-level sentiment analysis on OCR-degraded Slovene (selecting the GaMS3-12B-Instruct model), use that model for large-scale sentiment classification of mentions of collective identities, construct NER-based entity graphs linking groups and places, and analyse these graphs using quantitative network analysis combined with qualitative critical discourse analysis.
AI.result: They find shared concerns but clear ideological differences between the conservative-Catholic and liberal-progressive newspapers; identify that the chosen GaMS3-12B-Instruct model performs best but is biased toward neutral sentiment; show that different collective identities are represented with varying degrees of neutral versus evaluative/conflictual language; and map relational patterns between collective identities and places through NER graphs that reveal intertwined political and socionomic identities.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that combining topic modelling, LLM-based sentiment analysis, and entity-graph methods with critical discourse analysis is an effective strategy for investigating representations of collective identities in noisy historical newspaper corpora, demonstrating the value of mixed computational–interpretive approaches in digital humanities research.

title: On the Foundations of Trustworthy Artificial Intelligence
summary:
We prove that platform-deterministic inference is necessary and sufficient for
  trustworthy AI. We formalize this as the Determinism Thesis and introduce trust
  entropy to quantify the cost of non-determinism, proving that verification failure
  probability equals 1 - 2^{-H_T} exactly. We prove a Determinism-Verification
  Collapse: verification under determinism requires O(1) hash comparison; without it,
  the verifier faces an intractable membership problem. IEEE 754 floating-point
  arithmetic fundamentally violates the determinism requirement. We resolve this by
  constructing a pure integer inference engine that achieves bitwise identical output
  across ARM and x86. In 82 cross-architecture tests on models up to 6.7B parameters,
  we observe zero hash mismatches. Four geographically distributed nodes produce
  identical outputs, verified by 356 on-chain attestation transactions. Every major
  trust property of AI systems (fairness, robustness, privacy, safety, alignment)
  presupposes platform determinism. Our system, 99,000 lines of Rust deployed across
  three continents, establishes that AI trust is a question of arithmetic.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that having AI inference behave deterministically across platforms is both necessary and sufficient for trustworthy AI, and it presents a deterministic integer-based engine that achieves bitwise-identical outputs across different hardware.
AI.motivation: Existing AI systems often produce different outputs across hardware and runs due to non-determinism (e.g., floating-point behavior), undermining trust, verifiability, and key properties like fairness and safety. There is no clear theoretical framework quantifying how determinism underpins trust, nor a practical demonstration of cross-platform bitwise-identical inference at scale.
AI.method: The authors formalize a Determinism Thesis stating that platform-level determinism is equivalent to trustworthy AI. They define a metric called trust entropy H_T to quantify non-determinism and prove that the failure probability of verification is exactly 1 - 2^{-H_T}. They show a Determinism-Verification Collapse: under determinism, verification is reduced to constant-time hash comparisons, while without it the verifier must solve an intractable membership problem. Observing that IEEE 754 floating-point arithmetic breaks determinism, they design and implement a purely integer-based inference engine that yields identical outputs across ARM and x86 architectures. They test this engine across architectures and locations, and validate equality via hash checks and on-chain attestations.
AI.result: The integer-based inference engine produces bitwise-identical outputs across ARM and x86 in 82 cross-architecture tests on models up to 6.7B parameters, with zero hash mismatches. Four geographically distributed nodes all produce identical outputs, supported by 356 on-chain attestation transactions. Theoretical results link verification failure probability to trust entropy and show that determinism reduces verification to O(1) hash comparison, while non-determinism makes verification intractable.
AI.conclusion: Platform determinism is both necessary and sufficient for trustworthy AI, and all major trust properties—including fairness, robustness, privacy, safety, and alignment—depend on it. Non-deterministic floating-point arithmetic is fundamentally incompatible with such trust, but a carefully designed integer-based inference engine can restore determinism across heterogeneous hardware. The authors conclude that the core of AI trust reduces to ensuring deterministic arithmetic, and they demonstrate its practicality with a large Rust implementation deployed globally.

title: Closing the Confidence-Faithfulness Gap in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) tend to verbalize confidence scores that are largely detached from their actual accuracy, yet the geometric relationship governing this behavior remain poorly understood. In this work, we present a mechanistic interpretability analysis of verbalized confidence, using linear probes and contrastive activation addition (CAA) steering to show that calibration and verbalized confidence signals are encoded linearly but are orthogonal to one another -- a finding consistent across three open-weight models and four datasets. Interestingly, when models are prompted to simultaneously reason through a problem and verbalize a confidence score, the reasoning process disrupts the verbalized confidence direction, exacerbating miscalibration. We term this the "Reasoning Contamination Effect." Leveraging this insight, we introduce a two-stage adaptive steering pipeline that reads the model's internal accuracy estimate and steers verbalized output to match it, substantially improving calibration alignment across all evaluated models.

AI.tldr: Study analyzes why large language models’ spoken confidence scores are poorly calibrated and proposes a method to align them with internal accuracy estimates.
AI.motivation: LLMs often give confidence scores that do not match their true accuracy, and the geometric/representation-level reasons for this miscalibration are not well understood.
AI.method: Use mechanistic interpretability tools (linear probes and contrastive activation addition steering) across multiple open-weight models and datasets to identify how calibration and verbalized confidence are encoded in activations, and then design a two-stage adaptive steering pipeline that reads internal accuracy estimates and steers output confidence accordingly.
AI.result: Find that internal calibration signals and verbalized confidence are encoded linearly but in orthogonal directions; reasoning steps interfere with the confidence direction and worsen miscalibration (“Reasoning Contamination Effect”). The proposed adaptive steering pipeline uses the internal accuracy signal to adjust verbalized confidence, substantially improving calibration across evaluated models.
AI.conclusion: Model activations contain a linearly accessible internal estimate of accuracy that is distinct from and orthogonal to the direction controlling verbalized confidence; reasoning can corrupt this confidence direction. By reading and steering along these directions, one can significantly improve alignment between stated confidence and true accuracy, leading to better-calibrated LLMs.

title: LogitScope: A Framework for Analyzing LLM Uncertainty Through Information Metrics
summary: Understanding and quantifying uncertainty in large language model (LLM) outputs is critical for reliable deployment. However, traditional evaluation approaches provide limited insight into model confidence at individual token positions during generation. To address this issue, we introduce LogitScope, a lightweight framework for analyzing LLM uncertainty through token-level information metrics computed from probability distributions. By measuring metrics such as entropy and varentropy at each generation step, LogitScope reveals patterns in model confidence, identifies potential hallucinations, and exposes decision points where models exhibit high uncertainty, all without requiring labeled data or semantic interpretation. We demonstrate LogitScope's utility across diverse applications including uncertainty quantification, model behavior analysis, and production monitoring. The framework is model-agnostic, computationally efficient through lazy evaluation, and compatible with any HuggingFace model, enabling both researchers and practitioners to inspect LLM behavior during inference.

AI.tldr: The paper presents LogitScope, a framework that analyzes token-level uncertainty in LLM outputs using information-theoretic metrics from the model’s own logits, enabling better understanding and monitoring of model behavior without labeled data.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM evaluation methods mainly provide sequence-level or task-level scores and do not expose how confident the model is at each token during generation. This lack of fine-grained, interpretable uncertainty signals makes it hard to detect hallucinations, understand decision points, and monitor deployed systems. The authors aim to provide a systematic way to inspect and quantify token-level uncertainty directly from model probability distributions.
AI.method: The authors propose LogitScope, a lightweight, model-agnostic framework that hooks into LLM inference to extract token-level probability distributions (logits) at each generation step. It then computes information metrics such as entropy and varentropy per token position. These metrics are used to visualize and analyze patterns of uncertainty, such as spikes in entropy indicating decision points or potential hallucinations. The framework uses lazy evaluation for computational efficiency and is built to work seamlessly with HuggingFace models.
AI.result: Using LogitScope on various LLMs and tasks, the authors show that token-level entropy and related metrics correlate with intuitive notions of confidence: low entropy in unambiguous or formulaic regions, and high entropy around uncertain or creative outputs. They demonstrate examples where high-uncertainty spans align with likely hallucinations or unstable generations, and show how the tool can be plugged into monitoring pipelines to flag risky outputs. Empirical case studies illustrate its use for uncertainty quantification, detailed behavior analysis, and production monitoring scenarios.
AI.conclusion: LogitScope provides a practical, computationally efficient, and model-agnostic way to analyze LLM uncertainty at the token level using information-theoretic metrics derived from logits. It enables deeper understanding of LLM behavior, supports detection of potential hallucinations and critical decision points, and can be integrated into research workflows and production monitoring. The authors conclude that such token-level uncertainty inspection is a valuable complement to traditional evaluation methods, improving transparency and reliability of LLM deployments.

title: OMIND: Framework for Knowledge Grounded Finetuning and Multi-Turn Dialogue Benchmark for Mental Health LLMs
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown remarkable capabilities for complex tasks, yet adaptation in medical domain, specifically mental health, poses specific challenges. Mental health is a rising concern globally with LLMs having large potential to help address the same. We highlight three primary challenges for LLMs in mental health - lack of high quality interpretable and knowledge grounded training data; training paradigms restricted to core capabilities, and evaluation of multi turn dialogue settings. Addressing it, we present oMind framework which includes training and aligning LLM agents for diverse capabilities including conversations; high quality ~164k multi-task SFT dataset, as a result of our generation pipeline based on Structured Knowledge retrieval, LLM based pruning, and review actions. We also introduce oMind-Chat - a novel multi turn benchmark dataset with expert annotated turn level and conversation level rubrics. Our diverse experiments on both core capabilities and conversations shows oMind LLMs consistently outperform baselines. oMind-LLM also shows significantly better reasoning with up to 80% win rate.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces oMind, a framework and dataset suite to train, align, and evaluate large language models for mental health applications, demonstrating improved reasoning and conversational performance over baselines.
AI.motivation: While LLMs excel in many tasks, adapting them safely and effectively to mental health support is difficult due to lack of high-quality, interpretable, and knowledge-grounded training data; limited training paradigms focusing mostly on core capabilities (not rich counseling-like interactions); and inadequate evaluation methods for multi-turn mental health dialogues. There is a growing global mental health burden and a parallel interest in using LLMs to help, but current resources and benchmarks are insufficient to ensure reliability and safety.
AI.method: The authors propose the oMind framework, which encompasses: (1) creating a ~164k example multi-task supervised fine-tuning (SFT) dataset via a pipeline that uses structured knowledge retrieval, LLM-based pruning, and human or expert review; (2) training and aligning LLM agents on this dataset for diverse capabilities including mental-health-oriented conversations; and (3) constructing oMind-Chat, a new multi-turn benchmark for mental health dialogue with expert-annotated rubrics at both the turn and full-conversation levels to evaluate models more holistically.
AI.result: Models trained in the oMind framework (oMind-LLMs) achieve consistently better performance than baseline LLMs across both core capabilities and conversational tasks. In particular, they report substantially improved reasoning, with up to an 80% win rate in comparative evaluations.
