The new benchmark and dataset also enable more fine-grained assessment of conversational quality in mental health contexts.
AI.conclusion: The oMind framework, together with its high-quality multi-task SFT dataset and the oMind-Chat benchmark, provides an effective path to train and evaluate LLMs for mental health applications. By addressing data quality, training diversity, and multi-turn evaluation, oMind-LLMs outperform baselines and show stronger reasoning capabilities, suggesting that carefully designed domain-specific pipelines can significantly improve LLM performance in sensitive domains like mental health.

title: Decoding Market Emotions in Cryptocurrency Tweets via Predictive Statement Classification with Machine Learning and Transformers
summary: The growing prominence of cryptocurrencies has triggered widespread public engagement and increased speculative activity, particularly on social media platforms. This study introduces a novel classification framework for identifying predictive statements in cryptocurrency-related tweets, focusing on five popular cryptocurrencies: Cardano, Matic, Binance, Ripple, and Fantom. The classification process is divided into two stages: Task 1 involves binary classification to distinguish between Predictive and Non-Predictive statements. Tweets identified as Predictive proceed to Task 2, where they are further categorized as Incremental, Decremental, or Neutral. To build a robust dataset, we combined manual and GPT-based annotation methods and utilized SenticNet to extract emotion features corresponding to each prediction category. To address class imbalance, GPT-generated paraphrasing was employed for data augmentation. We evaluated a wide range of machine learning, deep learning, and transformer-based models across both tasks. The results show that GPT-based balancing significantly enhanced model performance, with transformer models achieving the highest F1-score in Task 1, while traditional machine learning models performed best in Task 2. Furthermore, our emotion analysis revealed distinct emotional patterns associated with each prediction category across the different cryptocurrencies.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a two-stage framework to detect and further classify predictive statements in cryptocurrency tweets, leveraging a hybrid of manual and GPT-based annotation, data augmentation, and emotion features to improve model performance.
AI.motivation: Cryptocurrency discussions on social media are heavily speculative and often contain informal predictions, yet there is no dedicated framework to automatically detect and categorize these predictive statements for analysis or downstream tasks like risk monitoring, sentiment tracking, or market intelligence.
AI.method: The authors build a dataset of tweets for five major cryptocurrencies (Cardano, Matic, Binance, Ripple, Fantom), using a two-step annotation pipeline that combines manual labeling with GPT-assisted annotation and paraphrasing for class balancing. They first classify tweets as Predictive vs. Non-Predictive (Task 1), and then split Predictive tweets into Incremental, Decremental, or Neutral (Task 2). Emotion features are extracted via SenticNet for each prediction category. A variety of machine learning, deep learning, and transformer-based models are trained and evaluated on both tasks, with GPT-based paraphrasing used as data augmentation to handle class imbalance.
AI.result: GPT-based paraphrasing for balancing significantly improves model performance in both classification tasks. Transformer models reach the highest F1-scores in the binary Predictive vs. Non-Predictive task, while traditional machine learning methods outperform more complex models in the finer-grained three-way classification of Incremental, Decremental, and Neutral predictions. Emotion analysis uncovers clear, distinct emotional signatures associated with each prediction type for different cryptocurrencies.
AI.conclusion: A two-stage predictive-statement classification framework for cryptocurrency tweets is effective and can be substantially improved via GPT-based data augmentation and emotion features. Different model families are optimal for different stages (transformers for detection, traditional ML for fine-grained categorization), and predictive categories are associated with distinct emotional patterns, which could be useful for understanding speculative behavior and building downstream monitoring tools.

title: Do LLMs Know What They Know? Measuring Metacognitive Efficiency with Signal Detection Theory
comment: 12 pages, 3 figures, 7 tables. Pre-registered; code and data at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/sdt_calibration
summary: Standard evaluation of LLM confidence relies on calibration metrics (ECE, Brier score) that conflate two distinct capacities: how much a model knows (Type-1 sensitivity) and how well it knows what it knows (Type-2 metacognitive sensitivity). We introduce an evaluation framework based on Type-2 Signal Detection Theory that decomposes these capacities using meta-d' and the metacognitive efficiency ratio M-ratio. Applied to four LLMs (Llama-3-8B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3, Llama-3-8B-Base, Gemma-2-9B-Instruct) across 224,000 factual QA trials, we find: (1) metacognitive efficiency varies substantially across models even when Type-1 sensitivity is similar -- Mistral achieves the highest d' but the lowest M-ratio; (2) metacognitive efficiency is domain-specific, with different models showing different weakest domains, invisible to aggregate metrics; (3) temperature manipulation shifts Type-2 criterion while meta-d' remains stable for two of four models, dissociating confidence policy from metacognitive capacity; (4) AUROC_2 and M-ratio produce fully inverted model rankings, demonstrating these metrics answer fundamentally different evaluation questions. The meta-d' framework reveals which models "know what they don't know" versus which merely appear well-calibrated due to criterion placement -- a distinction with direct implications for model selection, deployment, and human-AI collaboration. Pre-registered analysis; code and data publicly available.

AI.tldr: They propose a new way to evaluate how well language models’ confidence matches their actual knowledge by separating raw task ability from metacognitive skill, showing that models with similar accuracy can differ a lot in “knowing what they don’t know.”
AI.motivation: Existing calibration metrics (like ECE and Brier score) mix together two things: (a) whether a model’s answers are correct and (b) whether its confidence accurately tracks its correctness. This makes it hard to tell if a model is truly metacognitively competent or just looks calibrated because of how it sets its confidence threshold. The authors want a framework that cleanly separates these components so we can better compare models, understand domain-specific weaknesses, and make safer deployment decisions.
AI.method: They adopt Type-2 Signal Detection Theory from cognitive psychology to evaluate language model confidence. Using factual QA tasks (224k trials) for four LLMs (Llama-3-8B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3, Llama-3-8B-Base, Gemma-2-9B-Instruct), they compute Type-1 sensitivity (d’, task accuracy) and Type-2 measures: meta-d’ (how well confidence discriminates correct vs incorrect answers) and M-ratio (meta-d’ divided by d’, i.e., metacognitive efficiency). They further analyze domain-specific performance, manipulate sampling temperature to examine effects on confidence criterion vs metacognitive capacity, and compare rankings under AUROC_2 vs M-ratio.
AI.result: They find that models with similar task accuracy can have very different metacognitive efficiency: for example, Mistral attains the highest d’ (best raw performance) but the lowest M-ratio (poorest metacognitive efficiency). Metacognitive efficiency varies by knowledge domain and these weaknesses are hidden by aggregate metrics. Adjusting temperature primarily shifts the confidence criterion (how liberally or conservatively the model expresses confidence) without changing meta-d’ for two of the four models, revealing a dissociation between confidence policy and underlying metacognitive capacity. They also show that AUROC_2 and M-ratio can yield completely inverted rankings of models, proving that these metrics capture different evaluative questions.
AI.conclusion: The Type-2 SDT/meta-d’ framework provides a more precise evaluation of LLM confidence by disentangling knowledge accuracy from metacognitive efficiency. It highlights that some models genuinely “know what they don’t know,” while others only appear well-calibrated due to how they place their confidence criterion. This has practical implications for choosing and deploying models, especially in settings requiring reliable self-assessment and human-AI collaboration; it also underscores the need to evaluate metacognition at a domain level rather than relying solely on global calibration metrics.

title: FinMCP-Bench: Benchmarking LLM Agents for Real-World Financial Tool Use under the Model Context Protocol
summary: This paper introduces \textbf{FinMCP-Bench}, a novel benchmark for evaluating large language models (LLMs) in solving real-world financial problems through tool invocation of financial model context protocols. FinMCP-Bench contains 613 samples spanning 10 main scenarios and 33 sub-scenarios, featuring both real and synthetic user queries to ensure diversity and authenticity. It incorporates 65 real financial MCPs and three types of samples, single tool, multi-tool, and multi-turn, allowing evaluation of models across different levels of task complexity. Using this benchmark, we systematically assess a range of mainstream LLMs and propose metrics that explicitly measure tool invocation accuracy and reasoning capabilities. FinMCP-Bench provides a standardized, practical, and challenging testbed for advancing research on financial LLM agents.

AI.tldr: The paper presents FinMCP-Bench, a new benchmark to evaluate how well large language models use financial tools (MCPs) to solve real-world financial tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM benchmarks rarely focus on realistic financial scenarios or on accurate tool invocation with financial model context protocols. There is a need for a standardized way to measure whether LLM-based financial agents can correctly call and sequence real financial tools in diverse, complex situations.
AI.method: The authors build FinMCP-Bench with 613 query–task samples covering 10 main and 33 sub-scenarios, mixing real and synthetic user queries. They integrate 65 real financial MCPs and design three sample types—single-tool, multi-tool, and multi-turn—to capture increasing task complexity. They then use this benchmark to evaluate multiple mainstream LLMs and define metrics that explicitly quantify tool invocation accuracy and reasoning ability.
AI.result: FinMCP-Bench successfully differentiates model performance across scenarios and complexity levels, revealing strengths and weaknesses of current LLMs in financial tool use and reasoning. The proposed metrics give a clearer view of how accurately models choose and invoke tools and how well they reason over tool outputs.
AI.conclusion: FinMCP-Bench offers a standardized, realistic, and challenging benchmark for financial LLM agents, enabling more precise evaluation of their tool-usage and reasoning, and supporting future progress in building robust financial AI systems.

title: Goodness-of-pronunciation without phoneme time alignment
summary: In speech evaluation, an Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) model often computes time boundaries and phoneme posteriors for input features. However, limited data for ASR training hinders expansion of speech evaluation to low-resource languages. Open-source weakly-supervised models are capable of ASR over many languages, but they are frame-asynchronous and not phonemic, hindering feature extraction for speech evaluation. This paper proposes to overcome incompatibilities for feature extraction with weakly-supervised models, easing expansion of speech evaluation to low-resource languages. Phoneme posteriors are computed by mapping ASR hypotheses to a phoneme confusion network. Word instead of phoneme-level speaking rate and duration are used. Phoneme and frame-level features are combined using a cross-attention architecture, obviating phoneme time alignment. This performs comparably with standard frame-synchronous features on English speechocean762 and low-resource Tamil datasets.

AI.tldr: They adapt weakly-supervised multilingual ASR models for use in speech evaluation, enabling phoneme-like features and competitive performance without frame-synchronous, phonemic ASR, thus supporting low-resource languages.
AI.motivation: Conventional speech evaluation relies on ASR models that provide frame-synchronous phoneme boundaries and posteriors, but training such ASR systems requires substantial transcribed data, which is scarce for many low-resource languages. Existing open-source weakly-supervised ASR models cover many languages but are not directly usable for speech evaluation because they operate in a frame-asynchronous manner and at non-phonemic units. There is a need to bridge this gap so that speech evaluation systems can be more easily extended to low-resource languages without building dedicated, fully supervised phonemic ASR models.
AI.method: The authors use open-source weakly-supervised ASR models to generate ASR hypotheses, then map these hypotheses to a phoneme confusion network to derive approximate phoneme posterior distributions. Instead of relying on fine-grained phoneme-level timing, they use word-level speaking rate and duration as temporal features. They further design a cross-attention-based architecture that fuses phoneme-level and frame-level features without requiring explicit phoneme time alignment, allowing the system to learn correspondences between acoustic frames and phoneme-like units automatically.
AI.result: The proposed method, which leverages weakly-supervised, frame-asynchronous ASR models and cross-attention-based feature fusion, achieves performance in speech evaluation tasks that is comparable to systems using traditional frame-synchronous, phonemic ASR features. This is demonstrated on both the English speechocean762 dataset and a low-resource Tamil dataset, indicating the approach generalizes across languages and is particularly beneficial where fully supervised ASR resources are limited.
AI.conclusion: By converting weakly-supervised ASR outputs into phoneme-like posteriors via confusion networks, shifting temporal analysis to the word level, and using cross-attention to align phoneme and frame-level features implicitly, the paper shows that effective speech evaluation is possible without conventional frame-synchronous phonemic ASR. This substantially lowers the resource and engineering barrier to deploying speech evaluation in low-resource languages while retaining competitive performance with traditional approaches.

title: Shopping with a Platform AI Assistant: Who Adopts, When in the Journey, and What For
summary: This paper provides some of the first large-scale descriptive evidence on how consumers adopt and use platform-embedded shopping AI in e-commerce. Using data on 31 million users of Ctrip, China's largest online travel platform, we study "Wendao," an LLM-based AI assistant integrated into the platform. We document three empirical regularities. First, adoption is highest among older consumers, female users, and highly engaged existing users, reversing the younger, male-dominated profile commonly documented for general-purpose AI tools. Second, AI chat appears in the same broad phase of the purchase journey as traditional search and well before order placement; among journeys containing both chat and search, the most common pattern is interleaving, with users moving back and forth between the two modalities. Third, consumers disproportionately use the assistant for exploratory, hard-to-keyword tasks: attraction queries account for 42% of observed chat requests, and chat intent varies systematically with both the timing of chat relative to search and the category of products later purchased within the same journey. These findings suggest that embedded shopping AI functions less as a substitute for conventional search than as a complementary interface for exploratory product discovery in e-commerce.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how consumers adopt and use an LLM-based shopping assistant embedded in a major travel platform, finding that it complements rather than replaces traditional search, especially for exploratory tasks.
AI.motivation: While much is known about who uses general-purpose AI tools and how, there is little large-scale evidence on the adoption and usage patterns of AI assistants embedded directly into e-commerce platforms. Understanding this is important for platform design, marketing, and the future of online search and recommendation systems.
AI.method: The authors use large-scale behavioral data from 31 million users of Ctrip, focusing on interactions with “Wendao,” an LLM-based AI assistant integrated into the platform. They conduct descriptive empirical analysis of user adoption patterns and within-journey behavior, comparing AI chat use to traditional search and purchase timing, and categorizing intents such as exploratory attraction queries.
AI.result: They find that: (1) Adoption is highest among older, female, and already highly engaged users, unlike the younger, male-skewed adoption of general-purpose AI. (2) AI chat is used in the same early, consideration-stage of purchase journeys as traditional search, with users often interleaving between chat and search. (3) Users rely on the assistant disproportionately for exploratory, hard-to-keyword tasks; attraction-related queries make up 42% of chat requests, and chat intent systematically varies with when it is used in the journey and what is ultimately purchased.
AI.conclusion: Embedded shopping AI is not primarily a substitute for conventional search; instead, it serves as a complementary interface that supports exploratory product discovery, particularly for complex or loosely specified travel needs.

title: To Write or to Automate Linguistic Prompts, That Is the Question
comment: 10 pages, to be submitted for EAMT 2026
summary: LLM performance is highly sensitive to prompt design, yet whether automatic prompt optimization can replace expert prompt engineering in linguistic tasks remains unexplored. We present the first systematic comparison of hand-crafted zero-shot expert prompts, base DSPy signatures, and GEPA-optimized DSPy signatures across translation, terminology insertion, and language quality assessment, evaluating five model configurations. Results are task-dependent. In terminology insertion, optimized and manual prompts produce mostly statistically indistinguishable quality. In translation, each approach wins on different models. In LQA, expert prompts achieve stronger error detection while optimization improves characterization. Across all tasks, GEPA elevates minimal DSPy signatures, and the majority of expert-optimized comparisons show no statistically significant difference. We note that the comparison is asymmetric: GEPA optimization searches programmatically over gold-standard splits, whereas expert prompts require in principle no labeled data, relying instead on domain expertise and iterative refinement.

AI.tldr: This paper compares automatic prompt optimization with expert hand-crafted prompts for LLM-based linguistic tasks.
AI.motivation: LLM outputs are highly sensitive to prompt design, but it is unclear whether automatic methods can match or replace human experts for linguistic tasks such as translation and language quality assessment.
AI.method: They systematically compare three prompting approaches—expert zero-shot prompts, base DSPy signatures, and GEPA-optimized DSPy signatures—across three tasks (translation, terminology insertion, and language quality assessment) and five model configurations, using statistical significance testing to evaluate differences.
AI.result: Performance differences are task- and model-dependent: in terminology insertion, optimized and manual prompts are mostly statistically indistinguishable; in translation, different approaches win on different models; in LQA, expert prompts detect errors better while optimization improves characterization. GEPA consistently improves minimal DSPy signatures, and in most expert vs. optimized comparisons there is no statistically significant difference.
AI.conclusion: Automatic GEPA-based optimization can often match expert prompt engineering in linguistic tasks, especially when starting from minimal DSPy signatures, though expert prompts retain advantages such as not requiring labeled data and leveraging domain expertise, making the comparison asymmetric.

title: Can MLLMs Read Students' Minds? Unpacking Multimodal Error Analysis in Handwritten Math
comment: Accepted by the 27th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Education (AIED'26)
summary: Assessing student handwritten scratchwork is crucial for personalized educational feedback but presents unique challenges due to diverse handwriting, complex layouts, and varied problem-solving approaches. Existing educational NLP primarily focuses on textual responses and neglects the complexity and multimodality inherent in authentic handwritten scratchwork. Current multimodal large language models (MLLMs) excel at visual reasoning but typically adopt an "examinee perspective", prioritizing generating correct answers rather than diagnosing student errors. To bridge these gaps, we introduce ScratchMath, a novel benchmark specifically designed for explaining and classifying errors in authentic handwritten mathematics scratchwork. Our dataset comprises 1,720 mathematics samples from Chinese primary and middle school students, supporting two key tasks: Error Cause Explanation (ECE) and Error Cause Classification (ECC), with seven defined error types. The dataset is meticulously annotated through rigorous human-machine collaborative approaches involving multiple stages of expert labeling, review, and verification. We systematically evaluate 16 leading MLLMs on ScratchMath, revealing significant performance gaps relative to human experts, especially in visual recognition and logical reasoning. Proprietary models notably outperform open-source models, with large reasoning models showing strong potential for error explanation. All evaluation data and frameworks are publicly available to facilitate further research.

AI.tldr: ScratchMath is a benchmark for explaining and classifying errors in authentic handwritten math scratchwork using multimodal large language models.
AI.motivation: Handwritten student scratchwork contains rich information for diagnosing reasoning processes and misconceptions, but it is hard to analyze automatically because of messy handwriting, complex layout, and varied solving strategies. Existing educational NLP mainly targets clean textual answers, and current multimodal LLMs focus on getting the right answer rather than understanding and explaining student errors. There is a need for a benchmark that captures the real-world complexity of handwritten work and evaluates models on educationally meaningful error analysis tasks.
AI.method: The authors construct ScratchMath, a curated dataset of 1,720 real handwritten math scratchwork samples from Chinese primary and middle school students. They define two tasks: Error Cause Explanation (free-form natural language explanations of why an error occurred) and Error Cause Classification (categorizing each error into one of seven error types). The dataset is annotated via a multi-stage human-machine collaborative pipeline with expert labeling, review, and verification to ensure quality. They then benchmark 16 state-of-the-art multimodal large language models on these tasks to assess their capabilities in visual understanding and logical reasoning for error diagnosis.
AI.result: Experiments show that all evaluated MLLMs lag well behind human experts in both recognizing and reasoning about errors in handwritten scratchwork. The models especially struggle with visual recognition of messy, varied handwriting and with the logical reasoning needed to infer underlying misconceptions from the work. Proprietary, closed-source models perform substantially better than open-source models, and larger reasoning-oriented models show relatively strong performance on the explanation task, but there remains a sizeable gap to human-level error diagnosis.
AI.conclusion: ScratchMath exposes current limitations of multimodal LLMs for fine-grained educational diagnosis from authentic handwritten student work. The benchmark demonstrates that strong visual reasoning on standard benchmarks does not translate directly into reliable error analysis in real classroom artifacts. By releasing the data and evaluation framework, the authors aim to catalyze research on models that can robustly perceive handwritten work, classify error types, and provide pedagogically meaningful explanations, ultimately enabling more effective personalized feedback in education.

title: Prompt Attack Detection with LLM-as-a-Judge and Mixture-of-Models
summary: Prompt attacks, including jailbreaks and prompt injections, pose a critical security risk to Large Language Model (LLM) systems. In production, guardrails must mitigate these attacks under strict low-latency constraints, resulting in a deployment gap in which lightweight classifiers and rule-based systems struggle to generalize under distribution shift, while high-capacity LLM-based judges remain too slow or costly for live enforcement. In this work, we examine whether lightweight, general-purpose LLMs can reliably serve as security judges under real-world production constraints. Through careful prompt and output design, lightweight LLMs are guided through a structured reasoning process involving explicit intent decomposition, safety-signal verification, harm assessment, and self-reflection. We evaluate our method on a curated dataset combining benign queries from real-world chatbots with adversarial prompts generated via automated red teaming (ART), covering diverse and evolving patterns. Our results show that general-purpose LLMs, such as gemini-2.0-flash-lite-001, can serve as effective low-latency judges for live guardrails. This configuration is currently deployed in production as a centralized guardrail service for public service chatbots in Singapore. We additionally evaluate a Mixture-of-Models (MoM) setting to assess whether aggregating multiple LLM judges improves prompt-attack detection performance relative to single-model judges, with only modest gains observed.

AI.tldr: The paper explores using lightweight, general-purpose LLMs as fast, low-cost security judges for detecting prompt attacks in real-world systems, showing they can be effective under production latency constraints.
AI.motivation: Existing guardrails face a tradeoff: simple classifiers and rules are fast but fail under distribution shift and evolving attacks, while powerful LLM judges are accurate but too slow or expensive for live production use. There is a practical deployment gap for robust, low-latency defenses against jailbreaks and prompt injection in real applications.
AI.method: The authors design structured prompts for lightweight LLMs that enforce a multi-step reasoning process: decomposing user intent, verifying safety signals, assessing potential harm, and performing self-reflection. They then evaluate these models as security judges on a curated dataset that merges benign queries from real-world chatbots with adversarial prompts created by automated red teaming. They also test a Mixture-of-Models (MoM) configuration that aggregates multiple LLM judges to compare performance with single-model judges.
AI.result: General-purpose lightweight LLMs, such as gemini-2.0-flash-lite-001, achieve strong performance as low-latency security judges, detecting prompt attacks effectively enough for deployment in production guardrails. The MoM approach yields only modest performance improvements over single-model judges, suggesting limited additional benefit from simple aggregation under the tested conditions.
AI.conclusion: Carefully prompted lightweight LLMs can close the deployment gap by acting as practical, low-latency security judges for LLM applications, and are viable for real-world centralized guardrail services (e.g., in Singapore public service chatbots). Combining multiple such judges provides only marginal gains, indicating that the primary leverage comes from structured reasoning prompts rather than model ensembling.

title: Cross-Preference Learning for Sentence-Level and Context-Aware Machine Translation
summary: Context-aware machine translation (MT) leverages document-level information, yet it does not consistently outperform sentence-level MT, as contextual signals are unevenly beneficial across sentences. Existing training objectives do not explicitly model this variability, limiting a model's ability to adaptively exploit context. In this paper, we propose Cross-Preference Learning (CPL), a preference-based training framework that explicitly captures the complementary benefits of sentence-level and context-aware MT. CPL achieves this by integrating both intra- and cross-condition preferences into the preference optimization objective. The introduction of intra- and cross-condition preferences provides explicit supervision on when and how contextual information improves translation quality. We validate the proposed approach on several public context-aware MT tasks using multiple models, including Qwen3-4B, Qwen3-8B, and Llama-3-8B. Experimental results demonstrate consistent improvements in translation quality and robustness across both input conditions, achieved without any architectural modifications.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Cross-Preference Learning (CPL), a preference-based training framework that makes context-aware machine translation reliably outperform sentence-level MT by learning when and how to use document-level context.
AI.motivation: Although context-aware (document-level) machine translation can use surrounding sentences, it does not reliably beat standard sentence-level MT because context is not always helpful and current training objectives do not explicitly model which sentences benefit from context. The authors want a method that lets models adaptively exploit context only when it improves translation, instead of naively always using it.
AI.method: They propose Cross-Preference Learning (CPL), a preference-based optimization framework. CPL builds a training objective that compares and ranks translations under two input conditions: sentence-level and context-aware. It incorporates (1) intra-condition preferences, which compare good vs. bad translations under the same condition (e.g., two context-aware outputs), and (2) cross-condition preferences, which compare outputs across the two conditions (sentence-level vs. context-aware). These preferences supervise the model on when context helps and how to adjust its behavior to benefit from it. Implementation is done on top of existing LLM-based MT models without changing their architectures.
AI.result: On several public context-aware MT benchmarks and with multiple backbone models (Qwen3-4B, Qwen3-8B, Llama-3-8B), CPL leads to consistent gains in translation quality and robustness for both sentence-only and context-aware inputs. The improvements are achieved without modifying the model architecture, indicating that the benefits come from the training framework itself.
AI.conclusion: CPL effectively captures the complementary strengths of sentence-level and context-aware MT by explicitly encoding intra- and cross-condition preferences in the training objective. This enables models to more reliably exploit document context only when it is beneficial, yielding consistent quality and robustness improvements across tasks and model sizes, without architectural changes.

title: The Anatomy of Uncertainty in LLMs
summary: Understanding why a large language model (LLM) is uncertain about the response is important for their reliable deployment. Current approaches, which either provide a single uncertainty score or rely on the classical aleatoric-epistemic dichotomy, fail to offer actionable insights for improving the generative model. Recent studies have also shown that such methods are not enough for understanding uncertainty in LLMs. In this work, we advocate for an uncertainty decomposition framework that dissects LLM uncertainty into three distinct semantic components: (i) input ambiguity, arising from ambiguous prompts; (ii) knowledge gaps, caused by insufficient parametric evidence; and (iii) decoding randomness, stemming from stochastic sampling. Through a series of experiments we demonstrate that the dominance of these components can shift across model size and task. Our framework provides a better understanding to audit LLM reliability and detect hallucinations, paving the way for targeted interventions and more trustworthy systems.

AI.tldr: They propose a new framework to break down large language model (LLM) uncertainty into three interpretable parts to better understand and improve model reliability.
AI.motivation: Existing uncertainty measures for LLMs give only one undifferentiated score or use the simple aleatoric vs. epistemic split, which do not provide clear, actionable guidance on how to improve the model and have been shown insufficient to explain LLM uncertainty.
AI.method: They introduce an uncertainty decomposition framework that separates overall LLM uncertainty into three semantic components—input ambiguity (due to unclear prompts), knowledge gaps (missing or weak parametric knowledge in the model), and decoding randomness (from stochastic sampling)—and run experiments across different model sizes and tasks to analyze how these components vary.
AI.result: Experiments show that the relative importance of the three uncertainty components changes depending on the model size and the specific task, revealing nuanced patterns not captured by single-score or two-way decompositions.
AI.conclusion: Decomposing LLM uncertainty into input ambiguity, knowledge gaps, and decoding randomness offers a more informative view of model reliability, helping audit hallucinations and enabling more targeted interventions to make LLM systems more trustworthy.

title: Probing the Lack of Stable Internal Beliefs in LLMs
comment: Accepted by NeurIPS 2025 Workshop Mexico City PersonaNLP
summary: Persona-driven large language models (LLMs) require consistent behavioral tendencies across interactions to simulate human-like personality traits, such as persistence or reliability. However, current LLMs often lack stable internal representations that anchor their responses over extended dialogues. This work explores whether LLMs can maintain "implicit consistency", defined as persistent adherence to an unstated goal in multi-turn interactions. We designed a 20-question-style riddle game paradigm where an LLM is tasked with secretly selecting a target and responding to users' guesses with "yes/no" answers. Through evaluations, we find that LLMs struggle to preserve latent consistency: their implicit "goals" shift across turns unless explicitly provided their selected target in context. These findings highlight critical limitations in the building of persona-driven LLMs and underscore the need for mechanisms that anchor implicit goals over time, which is a key to realistic personality modeling in interactive applications such as dialogue systems.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether LLMs can keep an unstated, internal goal consistent across a multi-turn interaction.
AI.motivation: Persona-driven LLMs are expected to show stable, human-like traits and goals over time, but current models often respond inconsistently across turns, undermining realistic personality simulation.
AI.method: The authors create a 20-questions-style riddle game where an LLM must secretly pick a target and answer user guesses with yes/no responses, and then evaluate how consistently the model adheres to its initial, implicit choice over multiple turns, with and without explicitly restating the target in context.
AI.result: LLMs frequently fail to maintain a stable, latent goal during the game, changing their implicit target over the course of the dialogue unless the target is explicitly kept in the context.
AI.conclusion: LLMs currently lack mechanisms to reliably preserve implicit, unspoken goals over time, posing a challenge for persona-driven applications and suggesting the need for architectures or algorithms that better anchor internal goals across interactions.

title: Rethinking Failure Attribution in Multi-Agent Systems: A Multi-Perspective Benchmark and Evaluation
summary: Failure attribution is essential for diagnosing and improving multi-agent systems (MAS), yet existing benchmarks and methods largely assume a single deterministic root cause for each failure. In practice, MAS failures often admit multiple plausible attributions due to complex inter-agent dependencies and ambiguous execution trajectories. We revisit MAS failure attribution from a multi-perspective standpoint and propose multi-perspective failure attribution, a practical paradigm that explicitly accounts for attribution ambiguity. To support this setting, we introduce MP-Bench, the first benchmark designed for multi-perspective failure attribution in MAS, along with a new evaluation protocol tailored to this paradigm. Through extensive experiments, we find that prior conclusions suggesting LLMs struggle with failure attribution are largely driven by limitations in existing benchmark designs. Our results highlight the necessity of multi-perspective benchmarks and evaluation protocols for realistic and reliable MAS debugging.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new paradigm and benchmark for attributing failures in multi-agent systems (MAS) that explicitly allows multiple plausible causes, arguing that existing single-cause benchmarks misrepresent large language models’ capabilities in debugging such systems.
AI.motivation: Existing MAS failure attribution research typically assumes each failure has a single deterministic root cause. However, real-world MAS exhibit complex inter-agent dependencies and ambiguous execution paths, so multiple attributions can be simultaneously reasonable. Benchmarks and evaluations built on a single-cause assumption may therefore misjudge both systems’ behavior and LLMs’ diagnostic capabilities, motivating a more realistic, multi-perspective formulation.
AI.method: The authors redefine failure attribution in MAS as a multi-perspective problem where multiple plausible attributions may coexist. They operationalize this by (1) formulating the multi-perspective failure attribution paradigm, (2) constructing MP-Bench, a benchmark specifically built to capture attribution ambiguity in MAS scenarios, and (3) designing a new evaluation protocol that can score models under this multi-perspective setting instead of enforcing a single ground-truth cause.
AI.result: Using MP-Bench and the new evaluation protocol, the authors run extensive experiments with large language models on MAS failure attribution tasks. They observe that many earlier negative conclusions about LLMs’ attribution abilities are largely artifacts of restrictive, single-cause benchmark designs. Under the multi-perspective setting, LLMs demonstrate stronger and more nuanced diagnostic performance than previously reported.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that realistic MAS debugging requires acknowledging and modeling attribution ambiguity. Single-cause benchmarks can be misleading about model capabilities, especially for LLM-based tools. Multi-perspective benchmarks like MP-Bench and appropriate evaluation protocols are therefore necessary to obtain reliable assessments and to guide the development of effective MAS debugging methods.

title: A Catalog of Basque Dialectal Resources: Online Collections and Standard-to-Dialectal Adaptations
summary: Recent research on dialectal NLP has identified data scarcity as a primary limitation. To address this limitation, this paper presents a catalog of contemporary Basque dialectal data and resources, offering a systematic and comprehensive compilation of the dialectal data currently available in Basque. Two types of data sources have been distinguished: online data originally written in some dialect, and standard-to-dialect adapted data. The former includes all dialectal data that can be found online, such as news and radio sites, informal tweets, as well as online resources such as dictionaries, atlases, grammar rules, or videos. The latter consists of data that has been adapted from the standard variety to dialectal varieties, either manually or automatically. Regarding the manual adaptation, the test split of the XNLI Natural Language Inference dataset was manually adapted into three Basque dialects: Western, Central, and Navarrese-Lapurdian, yielding a high-quality parallel gold standard evaluation dataset. With respect to the automatic dialectal adaptation, the automatically adapted physical commonsense dataset (BasPhyCowest) underwent additional manual evaluation by native speakers to assess its quality and determine whether it could serve as a viable substitute for full manual adaptation (i.e., silver data creation).

AI.tldr: The paper compiles and evaluates Basque dialectal resources to alleviate data scarcity in dialectal NLP.
AI.motivation: Dialectal NLP for Basque is hindered by a lack of systematically collected, high-quality dialectal data, limiting model development and evaluation. The authors aim to provide a structured overview and new resources to support research on Basque dialects.
AI.method: They build a catalog that distinguishes between (1) naturally occurring online dialectal data (news, radio, tweets, dictionaries, atlases, grammar materials, videos) and (2) standard Basque texts adapted into dialects, either manually or automatically. They manually adapt the XNLI test split into three major Basque dialects, and they automatically adapt a physical commonsense dataset (BasPhyCowest), followed by human evaluation by native speakers.
AI.result: The work yields: (a) a systematic catalog of existing Basque dialectal resources, (b) a high-quality gold standard parallel evaluation set based on XNLI in three dialects, and (c) an automatically adapted commonsense dataset whose quality is assessed by native speakers as potential silver data.
AI.conclusion: A comprehensive, organized set of Basque dialectal resources combined with newly created gold and silver datasets can mitigate data scarcity and provide a foundation for training and evaluating dialect-aware NLP models for Basque.

title: A Public Theory of Distillation Resistance via Constraint-Coupled Reasoning Architectures
summary: Knowledge distillation, model extraction, and behavior transfer have become central concerns in frontier AI. The main risk is not merely copying, but the possibility that useful capability can be transferred more cheaply than the governance structure that originally accompanied it. This paper presents a public, trade-secret-safe theoretical framework for reducing that asymmetry at the architectural level. The core claim is that distillation becomes less valuable as a shortcut when high-level capability is coupled to internal stability constraints that shape state transitions over time. To formalize this idea, the paper introduces a constraint-coupled reasoning framework with four elements: bounded transition burden, path-load accumulation, dynamically evolving feasible regions, and a capability-stability coupling condition. The paper is intentionally public-safe: it omits proprietary implementation details, training recipes, thresholds, hidden-state instrumentation, deployment procedures, and confidential system design choices. The contribution is therefore theoretical rather than operational. It offers a falsifiable architectural thesis, a clear threat model, and a set of experimentally testable hypotheses for future work on distillation resistance, alignment, and model governance.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a theoretical, safety‑oriented architectural framework aimed at making knowledge distillation and model extraction less effective as cheap shortcuts for transferring AI capabilities, by tightly coupling capabilities to internal stability constraints over time.
AI.motivation: As AI capabilities advance, techniques like knowledge distillation and model extraction make it easier to copy or cheaply transfer useful capabilities from one model to another. The authors are concerned that this capability transfer can occur without also transferring the costly governance, oversight, and safety structures that originally accompanied the powerful system. This creates an asymmetry: it may become much cheaper to replicate capability than to replicate responsible control. The paper is motivated by the need for public, trade‑secret‑safe theory that can guide the design of architectures that are harder to distill or extract in a way that preserves full capability, thereby supporting safer model governance and alignment.
AI.method: The authors develop a formal, theoretical framework they call a constraint‑coupled reasoning framework. It has four key components: (1) bounded transition burden, which limits how much a model’s internal state can change between steps; (2) path‑load accumulation, which tracks how constraints or costs build up along a trajectory of internal states; (3) dynamically evolving feasible regions, where the set of allowable internal states changes over time depending on prior states and constraints; and (4) a capability‑stability coupling condition, which links high‑level performance capabilities to satisfying these internal stability constraints. They use this structure to articulate a formal threat model and generate experimentally testable hypotheses about when and how distillation becomes less effective as a shortcut.
AI.result: The paper does not provide empirical results or implementation details. Instead, its primary result is a public, trade‑secret‑safe theoretical framework for reasoning about and designing architectures where high‑level capabilities are inseparable from particular stability properties of internal state transitions. Within this framework, the authors argue that successful model distillation or extraction must not only match input‑output behavior but also reproduce key stability dynamics, which they claim is harder and costlier. They thus outline conditions under which distillation should face diminishing returns as a capability‑copying shortcut, and they specify how this connects to a concrete threat model around capability transfer without governance transfer.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that coupling capabilities to internal stability constraints at the architectural level can, in principle, reduce the value of knowledge distillation and related techniques as cheap shortcuts for transferring powerful capabilities without their accompanying governance structures. It emphasizes that this is a theoretical, falsifiable architectural thesis rather than an operational recipe. The authors present a clear threat model and propose that future work should test the framework’s hypotheses experimentally, refine the constraint‑coupled reasoning formalism, and explore how such designs can support more robust distillation resistance, better alignment, and stronger model governance without disclosing sensitive implementation details.

title: A Decade-Scale Benchmark Evaluating LLMs' Clinical Practice Guidelines Detection and Adherence in Multi-turn Conversations
summary: Clinical practice guidelines (CPGs) play a pivotal role in ensuring evidence-based decision-making and improving patient outcomes. While Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in healthcare scenarios, it is unclear to which extend LLMs could identify and adhere to CPGs during conversations. To address this gap, we introduce CPGBench, an automated framework benchmarking the clinical guideline detection and adherence capabilities of LLMs in multi-turn conversations. We collect 3,418 CPG documents from 9 countries/regions and 2 international organizations published in the last decade spanning across 24 specialties. From these documents, we extract 32,155 clinical recommendations with corresponding publication institute, date, country, specialty, recommendation strength, evidence level, etc. One multi-turn conversation is generated for each recommendation accordingly to evaluate the detection and adherence capabilities of 8 leading LLMs. We find that the 71.1%-89.6% recommendations can be correctly detected, while only 3.6%-29.7% corresponding titles can be correctly referenced, revealing the gap between knowing the guideline contents and where they come from. The adherence rates range from 21.8% to 63.2% in different models, indicating a large gap between knowing the guidelines and being able to apply them. To confirm the validity of our automatic analysis, we further conduct a comprehensive human evaluation involving 56 clinicians from different specialties. To our knowledge, CPGBench is the first benchmark systematically revealing which clinical recommendations LLMs fail to detect or adhere to during conversations. Given that each clinical recommendation may affect a large population and that clinical applications are inherently safety critical, addressing these gaps is crucial for the safe and responsible deployment of LLMs in real world clinical practice.

AI.tldr: The paper presents CPGBench, a large-scale benchmark and automatic framework to test how well LLMs can detect and follow clinical practice guidelines in multi-turn medical conversations.
AI.motivation: Clinical practice guidelines are central to safe, evidence-based care, but as LLMs enter healthcare, it is unclear whether they can correctly recognize and adhere to these guidelines during realistic, multi-turn interactions. Existing evaluations rarely focus specifically on guideline detection and adherence, or on the gap between knowing guideline content versus its source, and on the ability to apply it in conversation. A dedicated, large-scale, systematically constructed benchmark is needed to reveal these gaps and guide safer deployment of LLMs in clinical practice.
AI.method: The authors build CPGBench by collecting 3,418 CPG documents from 9 countries/regions plus 2 international bodies, covering 24 specialties and the past decade. From these, they extract 32,155 individual clinical recommendations, each labeled with metadata (publishing institution, date, country, specialty, recommendation strength, evidence level, etc.). For each recommendation, they automatically generate a corresponding multi-turn conversation scenario. Eight state-of-the-art LLMs are evaluated on two dimensions: (1) guideline detection—whether the model can correctly identify the relevant recommendation; and (2) guideline adherence—whether the model’s conversational responses follow the recommendation. They also test whether models can correctly reference the guideline titles. To validate the automatic pipeline, they run a human evaluation with 56 clinicians from diverse specialties, comparing human ratings to their automatic metrics.
AI.result: Across models, 71.1%–89.6% of recommendations are correctly detected, meaning the models often know the relevant guideline content. However, only 3.6%–29.7% of guideline titles are correctly referenced, indicating poor grounding in and citation of specific CPG sources. Guideline adherence is substantially lower than detection, with adherence rates from 21.8% to 63.2% depending on the model, showing that models frequently fail to apply guidelines correctly in multi-turn conversations even when they ostensibly know them. Human clinician evaluation supports the validity of the automatic assessment, confirming that CPGBench reliably reveals LLM failures in detection and adherence.
AI.conclusion: CPGBench is introduced as the first systematic benchmark explicitly targeting detection and adherence to clinical practice guidelines by LLMs in multi-turn conversations. The findings expose a pronounced gap between models’ apparent knowledge of guideline content and their ability to (a) accurately reference the underlying CPGs and (b) consistently apply recommendations in realistic dialogues. Given the safety-critical nature and population impact of each clinical recommendation, these shortcomings pose significant risks for real-world clinical deployment. The authors argue that improving guideline grounding and adherence—and using CPGBench to track progress—is essential for the safe, responsible use of LLMs in healthcare decision support.

title: System-Anchored Knee Estimation for Low-Cost Context Window Selection in PDE Forecasting
summary: Autoregressive neural PDE simulators predict the evolution of physical fields one step at a time from a finite history, but low-cost context-window selection for such simulators remains an unformalized problem. Existing approaches to context-window selection in time-series forecasting include exhaustive validation, direct low-cost search, and system-theoretic memory estimation, but they are either expensive, brittle, or not directly aligned with downstream rollout performance. We formalize explicit context-window selection for fixed-window autoregressive neural PDE simulators as an independent low-cost algorithmic problem, and propose \textbf{System-Anchored Knee Estimation (SAKE)}, a two-stage method that first identifies a small structured candidate set from physically interpretable system anchors and then performs knee-aware downstream selection within it. Across all eight PDEBench families evaluated under the shared \(L\in\{1,\dots,16\}\) protocol, SAKE is the strongest overall matched-budget low-cost selector among the evaluated methods, achieving 67.8\% Exact, 91.7\% Within-1, 6.1\% mean regret@knee, and a cost ratio of 0.051 (94.9\% normalized search-cost savings).

AI.tldr: They propose a low-cost method (SAKE) to choose the best context-window length for autoregressive neural PDE simulators, significantly cutting search cost while closely matching the performance of exhaustive selection across many PDE benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive neural PDE simulators require choosing how many past steps (context window L) to use, which strongly affects rollout performance. Existing ways to select L (exhaustive validation, heuristic search, or system-theoretic estimates) are costly, fragile, or not well-aligned with long-horizon rollout accuracy. There is no explicit, low-cost, principled procedure tailored to these simulators, so practitioners lack guidance for picking L efficiently and reliably.
AI.method: They formalize context-window selection for fixed-window autoregressive neural PDE simulators as a separate, low-cost algorithmic problem. They introduce SAKE (System-Anchored Knee Estimation), a two-stage procedure: (1) use physically interpretable system anchors to define a small, structured candidate set of context lengths; (2) perform a ‘knee-aware’ downstream selection within this candidate set, explicitly searching for the performance knee where adding more context yields diminishing returns. This design reduces evaluation cost while grounding the search in the system’s physical properties.
AI.result: Evaluated on eight PDEBench families under a shared protocol where context length L ranges from 1 to 16, SAKE outperforms other low-cost context-window selectors under matched budget. It attains 67.8% exact matches to the best L, 91.7% within ±1 of the best L, only 6.1% mean regret at the selected knee point, and a cost ratio of 0.051, corresponding to 94.9% savings in search cost compared to exhaustive methods.
AI.conclusion: SAKE provides an effective, low-cost, and principled way to choose context-window lengths for autoregressive neural PDE simulators. By anchoring candidate windows in system physics and using a knee-aware selection strategy, it closely approximates the performance of exhaustive search while using only about 5% of the search cost, establishing a strong general-purpose selector across diverse PDE benchmarks.

title: From Stateless to Situated: Building a Psychological World for LLM-Based Emotional Support
summary: In psychological support and emotional companionship scenarios, the core limitation of large language models (LLMs) lies not merely in response quality, but in their reliance on local next-token prediction, which prevents them from maintaining the temporal continuity, stage awareness, and user consent boundaries required for multi-turn intervention. This stateless characteristic makes systems prone to premature advancement, stage misalignment, and boundary violations in continuous dialogue. To address this problem, we argue that the key challenge in process-oriented emotional support is not simply generating natural language, but constructing a sustainably updatable external situational structure for the model. We therefore propose LEKIA 2.0, a situated LLM architecture that separates the cognitive layer from the executive layer, thereby decoupling situational modeling from intervention execution. This design enables the system to maintain stable representations of the user's situation and consent boundaries throughout ongoing interaction. To evaluate this process-control capability, we further introduce a Static-to-Dynamic online evaluation protocol for multi-turn interaction. LEKIA achieved an average absolute improvement of approximately 31% over prompt-only baselines in deep intervention loop completion. The results suggest that an external situational structure is a key enabling condition for building stable, controllable, and situated emotional support systems.

AI.tldr: LEKIA 2.0 is a situated LLM architecture that uses an external, updatable situational structure and separates cognition from execution to provide safer, more stable multi-turn emotional support.
AI.motivation: Existing LLMs, relying on local next-token prediction, struggle in psychological support settings because they lack temporal continuity, stage awareness, and persistent user-consent tracking. This statelessness causes premature advancement in interventions, misalignment with the user’s current stage, and violations of consent boundaries. The authors aim to address these limitations so LLMs can reliably handle multi-turn, process-oriented emotional support.
AI.method: The paper proposes LEKIA 2.0, a situated LLM architecture that explicitly separates a cognitive layer (for situational modeling) from an executive layer (for generating interventions). The system maintains an external, sustainably updatable situational structure that encodes the user’s state, progress through an emotional support process, and consent boundaries. They also design a Static-to-Dynamic online evaluation protocol tailored for multi-turn interactions, measuring the system’s ability to correctly complete deep intervention loops compared to prompt-only baselines.
AI.result: Using the proposed Static-to-Dynamic evaluation for multi-turn emotional support, LEKIA 2.0 shows about a 31% absolute improvement over prompt-only baselines on deep intervention loop completion, indicating substantially better process control and stage alignment during extended interactions.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that building an external, persistently updatable situational structure—combined with separating cognitive modeling from execution—is crucial for creating stable, controllable, and contextually grounded emotional support systems with LLMs. LEKIA 2.0 demonstrates that this architectural choice significantly improves multi-turn process control, suggesting a promising direction for future situated AI companions.

title: Translation or Recitation? Calibrating Evaluation Scores for Machine Translation of Extremely Low-Resource Languages
summary: The landscape of extremely low-resource machine translation (MT) is characterized by perplexing variability in reported performance, often making results across different language pairs difficult to contextualize. For researchers focused on specific language groups -- such as ancient languages -- it is nearly impossible to determine if breakthroughs reported in other contexts (e.g., native African or American languages) result from superior methodologies or are merely artifacts of benchmark collection. To address this problem, we introduce the FRED Difficulty Metrics, which include the Fertility Ratio (F), Retrieval Proxy (R), Pre-training Exposure (E), and Corpus Diversity (D) and serve as dataset-intrinsic metrics to contextualize reported scores. These metrics reveal that a significant portion of result variability is explained by train-test overlap and pre-training exposure rather than model capability. Additionally, we identify that some languages -- particularly extinct and non-Latin indigenous languages -- suffer from poor tokenization coverage (high token fertility), highlighting a fundamental limitation of transferring models from high-resource languages that lack a shared vocabulary. By providing these indices alongside performance scores, we enable more transparent evaluation of cross-lingual transfer and provide a more reliable foundation for the XLR MT community.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes FRED Difficulty Metrics to contextualize performance in extremely low-resource machine translation by quantifying dataset-intrinsic factors that affect results.
AI.motivation: Extremely low-resource MT shows high variability in reported performance, making it hard to compare methods or understand whether improvements are due to better models or easier datasets. For specific language groups, such as ancient or indigenous languages, it is unclear if reported breakthroughs in other settings can be transferred, because benchmark construction, data overlap, and pre-training exposure can heavily bias scores.
AI.method: The authors define four dataset-intrinsic difficulty indices – Fertility Ratio (F), Retrieval Proxy (R), Pre-training Exposure (E), and Corpus Diversity (D). These metrics quantify, respectively, tokenization coverage/fragmentation (fertility), train–test overlap (retrieval proxy), how much the language appears in model pre-training data (exposure), and the internal variety of the corpus (diversity). They compute these indices for different MT datasets and analyze how they correlate with reported translation performance across language pairs and settings.
AI.result: They find that much of the variability in low-resource MT results can be explained by differences in train-test overlap and pre-training exposure instead of genuine differences in model capability or research method. They also observe that extinct and non-Latin indigenous languages often have high token fertility, indicating poor tokenization coverage and lack of shared subword vocabulary with high-resource languages.
AI.conclusion: Reporting FRED Difficulty Metrics alongside MT scores allows the community to better interpret and compare results across benchmarks, clarifying when gains come from data artifacts versus real modeling advances. The work highlights fundamental limitations in transferring models from high-resource to certain low-resource languages and argues for standardized difficulty reporting to build a more reliable foundation for cross-lingual transfer research.

title: Mechanistically Interpreting Compression in Vision-Language Models
summary: Compressed vision-language models (VLMs) are widely used to reduce memory and compute costs, making them a suitable choice for real-world deployment. However, compressing these models raises concerns about whether internal computations and safety behaviors are preserved. In this work, we use causal circuit analysis and crosscoder-based feature comparisons to examine how pruning and quantization fundamentally change the internals across representative VLMs. We observe that pruning generally keeps circuit structure intact but rotates and attenuates internal features, while quantization modifies the circuits at a higher level yet leaves the surviving features better aligned. Leveraging this insight, we also introduce VLMSafe-420, a novel benchmark that pairs harmful inputs with matched benign counterfactuals across various safety categories. Our findings show that pruning causes a sharp drop in genuine refusal behavior, suggesting that the choice of compression has safety implications.

AI.tldr: They study how compressing vision-language models (VLMs) via pruning and quantization changes internal circuits and safety behavior, and propose a new safety benchmark showing pruning harms refusal behavior.
AI.motivation: Compressed VLMs are important for real-world deployment due to limited memory and compute, but it is unclear whether compression preserves internal computation structures and safety behaviors such as refusal of harmful requests.
AI.method: They apply causal circuit analysis and crosscoder-based feature comparison to compare internal representations of original vs. compressed VLMs under pruning and quantization. They analyze how circuit structure and features change, and create VLMSafe-420, a benchmark with paired harmful and benign counterfactual prompts across multiple safety types to systematically test refusal behavior.
AI.result: Pruning tends to preserve the overall circuit graph but rotates and weakens internal features; quantization changes circuits more globally but keeps surviving features better aligned with the original. On the VLMSafe-420 benchmark, pruned models show a pronounced decrease in genuine refusal behavior to harmful prompts.
AI.conclusion: Different compression methods alter VLM internals in distinct ways, and these changes materially affect safety behaviors. In particular, pruning can significantly degrade refusal behavior, so compression choices must be made and evaluated with safety impacts in mind, for which their VLMSafe-420 benchmark is a useful tool.

title: Comparing Natural and Synthetic Structured Data: A Study of the Passive Verb Alternation in French and Italian
comment: 13 pages, 8 figures, paper accepted at the Workshop on Structured Linguistic Data and Evaluation (SLiDE)
summary: This study compares the impact of natural and synthetic data on training and evaluating large language models (LLMs), using the case of passive verb alternation in French and Italian. We use Blackbird Language Matrices (BLMs), structured datasets designed to probe linguistic knowledge of underlying patterns across sentence sets. We compare structured templates instantiated with natural sentences extracted from Universal Dependencies to structured templates of synthetic sentences. Experiments show that while models achieve ceiling performance when trained and tested on synthetic datasets, they do not reliably generalize to natural sentences. In contrast, models trained on natural data exhibit robust performance across both natural and synthetic test suites, demonstrating their superior ability to capture abstract linguistic patterns. These results corroborate the value of natural data and of structured set ups in linguistic evaluation for probing LLMs' syntactic and semantic knowledge.

AI.tldr: The paper compares how natural vs synthetic data affect LLMs’ ability to learn and generalize a specific syntactic phenomenon (passive verb alternation) in French and Italian, showing natural data leads to more robust, abstract pattern learning.
AI.motivation: To understand whether synthetic, template-based datasets are sufficient for training and evaluating LLMs on fine-grained linguistic phenomena, or whether naturalistic data are needed to ensure robust generalization and truly abstract syntactic/semantic knowledge. The authors focus on passive verb alternation as a test case and use Blackbird Language Matrices as a controlled probing framework.
AI.method: They build Blackbird Language Matrices (BLMs) for passive verb alternation in French and Italian. These are structured sets of sentence templates capturing underlying linguistic patterns. They instantiate them in two ways: (1) synthetic sentences generated from templates; (2) natural sentences filling the same templates, extracted from Universal Dependencies treebanks. They then train LLMs under different regimes: trained & tested on synthetic; trained synthetic, tested natural; trained & tested on natural; trained natural, tested synthetic. They compare performance across these regimes to assess generalization and pattern abstraction.
AI.result: Models reach near-perfect (“ceiling”) performance when both training and testing are done on synthetic data. However, when models trained on synthetic data are evaluated on natural sentences, their performance drops and they fail to reliably generalize. In contrast, models trained on natural data perform well on both natural and synthetic test suites, indicating better abstraction of the underlying passive alternation patterns across surface variations.
AI.conclusion: Natural data provide crucial variability and cues that enable LLMs to acquire more abstract and robust syntactic and semantic representations, while purely synthetic, template-based training can overestimate model competence by encouraging superficial pattern-matching. Structured evaluation setups like Blackbird Language Matrices, populated with natural data, are valuable tools for probing what LLMs actually know about linguistic phenomena such as passive verb alternation in different languages.

title: MP-MoE: Matrix Profile-Guided Mixture of Experts for Precipitation Forecasting
summary: Precipitation forecasting remains a persistent challenge in tropical regions like Vietnam, where complex topography and convective instability often limit the accuracy of Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP) models. While data-driven post-processing is widely used to mitigate these biases, most existing frameworks rely on point-wise objective functions, which suffer from the ``double penalty'' effect under minor temporal misalignments. In this work, we propose the Matrix Profile-guided Mixture of Experts (MP-MoE), a framework that integrates conventional intensity loss with a structural-aware Matrix Profile objective. By leveraging subsequence-level similarity rather than point-wise errors, the proposed loss facilitates more reliable expert selection and mitigates excessive penalization caused by phase shifts. We evaluate MP-MoE on rainfall datasets from two major river basins in Vietnam across multiple horizons, including 1-hour intensity and accumulated rainfall over 12, 24, and 48 hours. Experimental results demonstrate that MP-MoE outperforms raw NWP and baseline learning methods in terms of Mean Critical Success Index (CSI-M) for heavy rainfall events, while significantly reducing Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) values. These findings highlight the framework's efficacy in capturing peak rainfall intensities and preserving the morphological integrity of storm events.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MP-MoE, a Matrix Profile-guided Mixture of Experts framework that improves tropical rainfall forecasts by combining standard intensity loss with a structure-aware similarity objective, reducing phase-shift penalties and better capturing storm morphology.
AI.motivation: Precipitation forecasting in tropical regions such as Vietnam is difficult because complex terrain and convective processes cause systematic errors in NWP rainfall forecasts. Standard data-driven post-processing approaches mostly use point-wise loss functions that heavily penalize small temporal misalignments between predicted and observed rainfall, leading to the double-penalty problem and degraded skill for intense, localized storms. There is a need for methods that account for the temporal structure and morphology of rainfall events rather than only point-wise accuracy.
AI.method: The authors propose Matrix Profile-guided Mixture of Experts (MP-MoE), a post-processing framework that augments conventional point-wise intensity loss with a structural Matrix Profile-based objective. The Matrix Profile measures subsequence-level similarity between time series, allowing the loss to focus on matching the shape and structure of rainfall events over windows instead of just individual time steps. This objective is used to guide the mixture-of-experts model so that the gating network selects experts that best capture event-level morphology, thereby mitigating excessive penalties from phase shifts between forecast and observation.
AI.result: On rainfall datasets from two major Vietnamese river basins, at multiple forecast horizons (1-hour intensity and 12, 24, 48-hour accumulations), MP-MoE outperforms the raw NWP outputs and standard learning-based post-processing baselines. It achieves higher Mean Critical Success Index (CSI-M) specifically for heavy rainfall events and yields substantially lower Dynamic Time Warping distances, indicating better alignment with observed temporal patterns and storm evolution.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating a Matrix Profile-based structural loss into a Mixture of Experts architecture yields more robust post-processed precipitation forecasts in tropical basins. By emphasizing subsequence-level similarity, the framework reduces the double-penalty effect from temporal shifts, enhances detection of peak rainfall, and preserves the morphological characteristics of storm events, making it a promising approach for operational rainfall forecasting in complex tropical environments.

title: MolQuest: A Benchmark for Agentic Evaluation of Abductive Reasoning in Chemical Structure Elucidation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) hold considerable potential for advancing scientific discovery, yet systematic assessment of their dynamic reasoning in real-world research remains limited. Current scientific evaluation benchmarks predominantly rely on static, single-turn Question Answering (QA) formats, which are inadequate for measuring model performance in complex scientific tasks that require multi-step iteration and experimental interaction. To address this gap, we introduce MolQuest, a novel agent-based evaluation framework for molecular structure elucidation built upon authentic chemical experimental data. Unlike existing datasets, MolQuest formalizes molecular structure elucidation as a multi-turn interactive task, requiring models to proactively plan experimental steps, integrate heterogeneous spectral sources (e.g., NMR, MS), and iteratively refine structural hypotheses. This framework systematically evaluates LLMs' abductive reasoning and strategic decision-making abilities within a vast and complex chemical space. Empirical results reveal that contemporary frontier models exhibit significant limitations in authentic scientific scenarios: notably, even state-of-the-art (SOTA) models achieve an accuracy of only approximately 50%, while the performance of most other models remains below the 30% threshold. This work provides a reproducible and extensible framework for science-oriented LLM evaluation, our findings highlight the critical gap in current LLMs' strategic scientific reasoning, setting a clear direction for future research toward AI that can actively participate in the scientific process.

AI.tldr: MolQuest is a new interactive benchmark that evaluates how well large language models can perform multi-step molecular structure elucidation using real experimental data.
AI.motivation: Existing scientific benchmarks for LLMs are mostly static, single-turn QA tasks and thus fail to capture the dynamic, multi-step reasoning and experimental decision-making required in real-world scientific research, especially in chemistry.
AI.method: The authors build MolQuest, an agent-based evaluation framework that frames molecular structure elucidation as a multi-turn interactive task. Models must plan experimental steps, query and integrate heterogeneous spectral data such as NMR and mass spectrometry, and iteratively refine candidate molecular structures. They then test multiple frontier LLMs in this environment, scoring them on accuracy and strategic reasoning performance over a large, realistic chemical space.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows that even state-of-the-art LLMs reach only about 50% accuracy on the MolQuest tasks, while most other models perform below 30%, exposing substantial gaps in their abilities to handle complex, interactive scientific reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: MolQuest offers a reproducible, extensible framework for evaluating LLMs in realistic scientific workflows, and the results emphasize that current LLMs lack robust strategic and abductive reasoning needed for active participation in scientific discovery, pointing to this as a key direction for future research.

title: Sparse Visual Thought Circuits in Vision-Language Models
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) improve interpretability in multimodal models, but it remains unclear whether SAE features form modular, composable units for reasoning-an assumption underlying many intervention-based steering methods. We test this modularity hypothesis and find it often fails: intervening on a task-selective feature set can modestly improve reasoning accuracy, while intervening on the union of two such sets reliably induces output drift (large unintended changes in predictions) and degrades accuracy, even under norm-matched perturbations. This non modular circuit interference is consistent with shared internal pathways where feature unions amplify activation shifts. We develop a reproducible causal pipeline to localize and test these sparse visual thought circuits in Qwen3-VL-8B. On a controlled synthetic benchmark with seven task types and three difficulty levels, linear probes identify a mid decoder locus for task type information. We train SAEs at this layer, construct task-selective sets via an explicit rule, and perform inference time scaling and ablation while quantifying accuracy and drift. Our findings-validated with bootstrapped subsamples and permutation controls, and replicated across multiple VLM families and five diverse datasets clarify the boundaries of SAE feature composability and provide a rigorous diagnostic framework for more reliable VLM control.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether features learned by sparse autoencoders in multimodal models act as modular, composable units for reasoning, and finds that combining interventions on multiple feature sets often causes harmful interference and output drift.
AI.motivation: Sparse autoencoders are widely used to interpret and steer large multimodal models under the assumption that their learned features are modular and can be composed safely for control. However, it is unclear whether this assumption holds for complex reasoning behavior in vision-language models, which is crucial for building reliable steering and safety mechanisms.
AI.method: The authors analyze a vision-language model (Qwen3-VL-8B) on a synthetic benchmark with seven task types and three difficulty levels.
