They use linear probes to identify the layer where task-type information is concentrated, then train sparse autoencoders on that layer. They define task-selective feature sets via an explicit rule and perform inference-time interventions, including scaling and ablation of these sets, while measuring both task accuracy and unintended output drift. They test single-task interventions and interventions on unions of task-selective sets, using norm-matched perturbations, bootstrapping, and permutation controls, and replicate the analysis across multiple VLM architectures and real datasets.
AI.result: Intervening on features selective for a single task can modestly improve reasoning accuracy with limited side effects. However, when intervening on the union of two task-selective feature sets, the model frequently exhibits non-modular circuit interference: output drift increases substantially and accuracy degrades, even when total intervention norm is matched. This behavior suggests overlapping internal pathways where combined feature activations amplify shifts, violating simple modularity assumptions. The observed phenomena are robust across tasks, difficulty levels, model families, and both synthetic and real datasets.
AI.conclusion: SAE-derived features in vision-language models do not generally behave as modular, composable units for reasoning-based interventions. Combining task-selective feature interventions can introduce strong interference and harmful output drift, indicating that current assumptions about feature modularity for steering are often invalid. The paper contributes a causal analysis pipeline and diagnostic framework for localizing sparse ‘visual thought’ circuits and rigorously evaluating the reliability and side effects of SAE-based control methods in VLMs.

title: CRAFT: Grounded Multi-Agent Coordination Under Partial Information
summary: We introduce CRAFT, a multi-agent benchmark for evaluating pragmatic communication in large language models under strict partial information. In this setting, multiple agents with complementary but incomplete views must coordinate through natural language to construct a shared 3D structure that no single agent can fully observe. We formalize this problem as a multi-sender pragmatic reasoning task and provide a diagnostic framework that decomposes failures into spatial grounding, belief modeling and pragmatic communication errors, including a taxonomy of behavioral failure profiles in both frontier and open-weight models. Across a diverse set of models, including 8 open-weight and 7 frontier including reasoning models, we find that stronger reasoning ability does not reliably translate to better coordination: smaller open-weight models often match or outperform frontier systems, and improved individual communication does not guarantee successful collaboration. These results suggest that multi-agent coordination remains a fundamentally unsolved challenge for current language models. Our code can be found at https://github.com/csu-signal/CRAFT

AI.tldr: CRAFT is a benchmark to test how well multiple language model agents can coordinate via natural language when each has only partial information about a shared 3D structure.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations mostly test single-agent reasoning or communication, not realistic multi-agent coordination where agents have different, incomplete views of a task. The authors want a way to systematically measure whether language models can pragmatically communicate and collaborate when no single agent has all the information.
AI.method: They design CRAFT, a benchmark in which several agents, each seeing only part of a 3D scene, must talk to each other in natural language to jointly construct a target 3D structure that none fully observes. They formalize this as a multi-sender pragmatic reasoning problem and build a diagnostic framework that decomposes errors into spatial grounding, belief modeling, and pragmatic communication issues. They also define a taxonomy of failure behaviors and test a variety of open-weight and frontier LLMs, including ones specialized for reasoning.
AI.result: Across 8 open-weight and 7 frontier models, they observe that better single-agent reasoning does not consistently yield better multi-agent coordination. Smaller open-weight models sometimes match or beat larger frontier models, and models that communicate well individually still often fail at true collaborative success. They categorize common failure patterns under their diagnostic framework.
AI.conclusion: Current large language models, even strong reasoning-oriented ones, do not reliably handle multi-agent coordination under partial information. The CRAFT benchmark and diagnostic tools highlight that pragmatic multi-agent communication and collaboration remain open challenges, suggesting future work should focus explicitly on these aspects rather than only improving individual reasoning ability.

title: ElephantBroker: A Knowledge-Grounded Cognitive Runtime for Trustworthy AI Agents
summary: Large Language Model based agents increasingly operate in high stakes, multi turn settings where factual grounding is critical, yet their memory systems typically rely on flat key value stores or plain vector retrieval with no mechanism to track the provenance or trustworthiness of stored knowledge. We present ElephantBroker, an open source cognitive runtime that unifies a Neo4j knowledge graph with a Qdrant vector store through the Cognee SDK to provide durable, verifiable agent memory. The system implements a complete cognitive loop (store, retrieve, score, compose, protect, learn) comprising a hybrid five source retrieval pipeline, an eleven dimension competitive scoring engine for budget constrained context assembly, a four state evidence verification model, a five stage context lifecycle with goal aware assembly and continuous compaction, a six layer cheap first guard pipeline for safety enforcement, an AI firewall providing enforceable tool call interception and multi tier safety scanning, a nine stage consolidation engine that strengthens useful patterns while decaying noise, and a numeric authority model governing multi organization identity with hierarchical access control. Architectural validation through a comprehensive test suite of over 2,200 tests spanning unit, integration, and end to end levels confirms subsystem correctness. The modular design supports three deployment tiers, five profile presets with inheritance, multi gateway isolation, and a management dashboard for human oversight, enabling configurations from lightweight memory only agents to full cognitive runtimes with enterprise grade safety and auditability.

AI.tldr: ElephantBroker is an open-source cognitive runtime that combines a knowledge graph and vector store to give LLM agents durable, verifiable, and safety-aware long-term memory for high-stakes, multi-turn tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM agent memory is typically flat and unstructured (simple key–value or bare vector retrieval), providing no explicit tracking of information provenance, trustworthiness, or lifecycle. This is inadequate for high-stakes, long-running, or multi-actor environments where factual accuracy, safety, access control, and auditability are crucial. The paper is motivated by the need for a principled, production-ready cognitive runtime that integrates memory, verification, safety, and governance in a unified architecture.
AI.method: The authors design and implement ElephantBroker, which integrates a Neo4j knowledge graph with a Qdrant vector store via the Cognee SDK. They define a full cognitive loop with explicit stages: (1) hybrid retrieval from five sources; (2) an 11-dimensional scoring engine for context selection under a token budget; (3) a four-state evidence verification model; (4) a five-stage context lifecycle with goal-aware assembly and automatic compaction; (5) a six-layer, cheap-first safety guard pipeline plus an AI firewall that intercepts and regulates tool calls with multi-tier safety scanning; (6) a nine-stage consolidation engine to reinforce useful patterns and decay noise; and (7) a numeric authority model to handle multi-organization identity and hierarchical access control. They validate the architecture via a large automated test suite (2,200+ tests) covering unit, integration, and end-to-end behavior and support multiple deployment tiers and configuration profiles.
AI.result: The implemented ElephantBroker runtime offers: (a) unified, provenance-aware, graph-plus-vector memory for agents; (b) budget-aware, competitively scored retrieval from multiple memory sources; (c) explicit evidence verification states tied to a context lifecycle; (d) layered safety and tool-call governance including an AI firewall; (e) consolidation mechanisms that maintain long-term memory quality; and (f) fine-grained authority and access control for multi-organization settings. The system passes a comprehensive test suite that demonstrates internal correctness of its subsystems and supports several deployment configurations, from lightweight agents to enterprise-grade runtimes with isolation and dashboards for human oversight.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that robust agentic systems require more than simple vector databases: they need a full cognitive runtime that integrates memory, verification, safety, and governance. ElephantBroker provides such a runtime by fusing a knowledge graph with a vector store and layering on retrieval, scoring, verification, safety, consolidation, and authority models. Its modularity, testing, and deployment options suggest it can serve as a foundation for durable, auditable, and secure LLM agents in high-stakes, multi-turn applications.

title: When Hate Meets Facts: LLMs-in-the-Loop for Check-worthiness Detection in Hate Speech
summary: Hateful content online is often expressed using fact-like, not necessarily correct information, especially in coordinated online harassment campaigns and extremist propaganda. Failing to jointly address hate speech (HS) and misinformation can deepen prejudice, reinforce harmful stereotypes, and expose bystanders to psychological distress, while polluting public debate. Moreover, these messages require more effort from content moderators because they must assess both harmfulness and veracity, i.e., fact-check them. To address this challenge, we release WSF-ARG+, the first dataset which combines hate speech with check-worthiness information. We also introduce a novel LLM-in-the-loop framework to facilitate the annotation of check-worthy claims. We run our framework, testing it with 12 open-weight LLMs of different sizes and architectures. We validate it through extensive human evaluation, and show that our LLM-in-the-loop framework reduces human effort without compromising the annotation quality of the data. Finally, we show that HS messages with check-worthy claims show significantly higher harassment and hate, and that incorporating check-worthiness labels improves LLM-based HS detection up to 0.213 macro-F1 and to 0.154 macro-F1 on average for large models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces WSF-ARG+, the first dataset that jointly labels hate speech and check-worthy claims, and proposes an LLM-in-the-loop annotation framework that reduces human effort while maintaining high quality. It shows that hate speech containing check-worthy claims is more harassing and hateful, and that adding check-worthiness labels significantly improves LLM-based hate speech detection performance.
AI.motivation: Online hate is often embedded in seemingly factual claims that may be misleading or false, especially in coordinated harassment and propaganda. Current approaches typically treat hate speech and misinformation separately, which overlooks their interaction and increases the burden on moderators, who must evaluate both harmfulness and factuality. There is a lack of datasets and methods that jointly model hatefulness and check-worthiness, limiting the development of tools that can better support moderation and mitigate the harms of fact-like hateful content.
AI.method: The authors construct WSF-ARG+, a dataset where hate speech instances are enriched with labels indicating whether they contain check-worthy claims. To scale the annotation of check-worthiness, they design an LLM-in-the-loop framework: multiple open-weight LLMs (12 models of various sizes and architectures) are used to assist in identifying candidate check-worthy content, which is then validated and refined by human annotators. The framework is evaluated via extensive human assessment to ensure that the LLM assistance does not degrade label quality. Finally, they train LLM-based hate speech detectors with and without the additional check-worthiness labels to quantify the impact on detection performance.
AI.result: The LLM-in-the-loop annotation framework successfully reduces the amount of human effort required to label check-worthy claims in hate speech content, while maintaining annotation quality comparable to fully manual labeling. Empirical analyses show that hate speech messages containing check-worthy claims tend to exhibit significantly higher levels of harassment and hate. When used as an additional signal in LLM-based hate speech classifiers, check-worthiness labels lead to substantial performance gains, with improvements up to 0.213 macro-F1 and average gains of 0.154 macro-F1 for large language models.
AI.conclusion: Jointly modeling hate speech and check-worthiness is both feasible and beneficial. The proposed WSF-ARG+ dataset and the LLM-in-the-loop annotation framework enable scalable creation of high-quality annotations that capture both harmfulness and the need for fact-checking. Incorporating check-worthiness information into hate speech detection pipelines yields significantly better performance, especially for large LLMs, and reveals that fact-like hateful messages are particularly harmful. This work suggests that content moderation systems should explicitly account for the factuality dimension of hateful content to more effectively detect and mitigate harm.

title: When Sensing Varies with Contexts: Context-as-Transform for Tactile Few-Shot Class-Incremental Learning
summary: Few-Shot Class-Incremental Learning (FSCIL) can be particularly susceptible to acquisition contexts with only a few labeled samples. A typical scenario is tactile sensing, where the acquisition context ({\it e.g.}, diverse devices, contact state, and interaction settings) degrades performance due to a lack of standardization. In this paper, we propose Context-as-Transform FSCIL (CaT-FSCIL) to tackle the above problem. We decompose the acquisition context into a structured low-dimensional component and a high-dimensional residual component. The former can be easily affected by tactile interaction features, which are modeled as an approximately invertible Context-as-Transform family and handled via inverse-transform canonicalization optimized with a pseudo-context consistency loss. The latter mainly arises from platform and device differences, which can be mitigated with an Uncertainty-Conditioned Prototype Calibration (UCPC) that calibrates biased prototypes and decision boundaries based on context uncertainty. Comprehensive experiments on the standard benchmarks HapTex and LMT108 have demonstrated the superiority of the proposed CaT-FSCIL.

AI.tldr: They propose CaT-FSCIL, a method that explicitly models and compensates for acquisition context in few-shot class-incremental tactile sensing, improving robustness across devices and interaction conditions.
AI.motivation: Few-Shot Class-Incremental Learning (FSCIL) suffers in real tactile sensing scenarios because data are collected under diverse, poorly standardized acquisition contexts (different devices, contact states, interaction settings). These contextual variations significantly degrade performance when only a few labeled samples are available. Existing FSCIL methods often ignore or under-model such context, leading to biased representations and unstable decision boundaries across tasks and sessions. There is a need for a principled way to factor out and compensate for acquisition context so that learned representations and prototypes are more consistent and transferrable in tactile benchmarks such as HapTex and LMT108.
AI.method: They introduce Context-as-Transform FSCIL (CaT-FSCIL). First, they explicitly decompose acquisition context into (1) a structured, low-dimensional component that is strongly influenced by tactile interaction features; and (2) a high-dimensional residual component largely due to platform and device differences. For the structured part, they model context as an approximately invertible transformation (Context-as-Transform family) applied to features, and learn an inverse-transform canonicalization so that features from different contexts are mapped to a common canonical space. This inverse-transform is trained using a pseudo-context consistency loss that encourages consistency across pseudo-labeled or estimated contexts. For the residual context, they propose Uncertainty-Conditioned Prototype Calibration (UCPC), which uses context uncertainty estimates to adjust class prototypes and refine decision boundaries, mitigating prototype bias induced by unseen or poorly sampled contexts.
AI.result: On the tactile FSCIL benchmarks HapTex and LMT108, CaT-FSCIL outperforms competing baselines. The experiments demonstrate that explicitly modeling acquisition context as transforms plus residuals yields more robust few-shot class-incremental performance, especially when data come from heterogeneous devices and interaction conditions. Quantitative metrics (e.g., accuracy over increments) consistently show superior or state-of-the-art results for CaT-FSCIL compared with existing FSCIL methods without context modeling.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling tactile acquisition context within FSCIL as a combination of a low-dimensional transform and a high-dimensional residual, and handling them via inverse-transform canonicalization and uncertainty-conditioned prototype calibration, significantly improves robustness and performance on real tactile benchmarks. This suggests that context-aware FSCIL is a promising direction not just for tactile sensing but potentially for other modalities where acquisition context is highly variable and hard to standardize.

title: Separate Before You Compress: The WWHO Tokenization Architecture
comment: 17 pages, 1 figure, 8 tables. Tokenization Architecture including formal DFA definitions and regular expressions for Sinhala and Devanagari syllabification. Evaluation includes comparisons with OpenAI o200k-base, Llama-4-Scout, and DeepSeek-V3. Source code and datasets: https://github.com/remeinium/WWHO
summary: Current Large Language Models (LLMs) mostly use BPE (Byte Pair Encoding) based tokenizers, which are very effective for simple structured Latin scripts such as English. However, standard BPE tokenizers struggle to process complex Abugida scripts due to their structural complexity. The problem is that these tokenizers break complex conjuncts, which are multi-codepoint grapheme clusters, into meaningless sub-character units. This degrades the LLM's reasoning efficiency by forcing it to learn basic orthographic structures at inference time and raises inference costs, resulting in a significant "Token Tax" for the Global South. We propose a new three-layer architecture, the WWHO (Where-What-How Often), and an algorithm named SGPE (Syllable-aware Grapheme Pair Encoding) that separates the linguistic rules of the script from the statistical compression process while enabling seamless multilingual tokenization. Using Sinhala and Devanagari (Hindi/Sanskrit) as highly complex Abugida scripts, we trained WWHO on a cleaned 30-million-sentence dataset and evaluated on a 1,499,950-sentence test set. For Sinhala, SGPE achieves a Token to Word Ratio (TWR) of 1.274 with 4.83 characters per token, representing a 61.7 percent reduction in tokens compared to OpenAI's o200k base. For Hindi, it achieves a TWR of 1.181 (27.0 percent reduction vs o200k). On the mixed-script (Sinhala, Devanagari, and English) dataset, SGPE achieves an overall TWR of 1.240, representing token reductions of 36.7 percent, 39.6 percent, and 60.2 percent relative to o200k base, Llama 4 Scout, and DeepSeek V3, respectively. This effectively extends the usable context window by up to 4.38 times for these Abugida languages while ensuring a Linguistic Zero-Breakage Guarantee, which ensures that no valid syllable is ever split across multiple tokens.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new tokenizer architecture and algorithm tailored for complex Abugida scripts, drastically reducing token counts and avoiding splitting valid syllables, thereby cutting inference cost and extending effective context windows for languages like Sinhala and Hindi.
AI.motivation: Standard BPE-based tokenizers, optimized for Latin scripts, perform poorly on complex Abugida scripts (e.g., Sinhala, Devanagari) by breaking multi-codepoint grapheme clusters into arbitrary sub-character fragments. This causes inefficiency: the model must implicitly relearn orthographic rules during inference, increasing token counts, degrading reasoning efficiency, and imposing a higher economic and computational "token tax" on users of Global South languages. There is a need for a tokenizer that respects linguistic structure while still providing strong compression and multilingual compatibility.
AI.method: The authors propose a three-layer tokenization architecture called WWHO (Where-What-How Often) and a corresponding algorithm SGPE (Syllable-aware Grapheme Pair Encoding). WWHO explicitly separates (1) where segmentation is allowed (linguistic constraints), (2) what units are considered (syllable-grapheme clusters), and (3) how often units occur for statistical compression. SGPE applies these principles to learn token units that align with whole syllables/graphemes rather than arbitrary byte pairs. They train this tokenizer on a large, cleaned 30M-sentence corpus in Sinhala and Devanagari and evaluate it on a ~1.5M-sentence test set, including monolingual and mixed-script (Sinhala, Devanagari, English) scenarios. They then compare Token to Word Ratio (TWR) and token reductions against popular tokenizers (OpenAI o200k base, Llama 4 Scout, DeepSeek V3).
AI.result: For Sinhala, SGPE achieves a Token to Word Ratio (TWR) of 1.274 with an average of 4.83 characters per token, giving a 61.7% token reduction relative to OpenAI's o200k base. For Hindi (Devanagari), SGPE obtains a TWR of 1.181, corresponding to a 27.0% reduction vs o200k. On a mixed-script dataset (Sinhala, Devanagari, English), SGPE yields an overall TWR of 1.240 and reduces tokens by 36.7% vs o200k, 39.6% vs Llama 4 Scout, and 60.2% vs DeepSeek V3. These reductions translate into a practical extension of the effective context window by up to about 4.38× for Abugida languages while preserving linguistic integrity.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that BPE-style tokenization, when naively applied to complex Abugida scripts, is structurally mismatched and leads to unnecessary token inflation and learning burden on LLMs. By decoupling linguistic rules from statistical token learning through the WWHO framework and SGPE algorithm, tokenization can respect syllable boundaries (Linguistic Zero-Breakage Guarantee) while achieving substantially better compression. This makes LLMs more efficient and cost-effective for Abugida languages and demonstrates that script-aware tokenization is critical for equitable access and performance across diverse writing systems.

title: RubricEval: A Rubric-Level Meta-Evaluation Benchmark for LLM Judges in Instruction Following
summary: Rubric-based evaluation has become a prevailing paradigm for evaluating instruction following in large language models (LLMs). Despite its widespread use, the reliability of these rubric-level evaluations remains unclear, calling for meta-evaluation. However, prior meta-evaluation efforts largely focus on the response level, failing to assess the fine-grained judgment accuracy that rubric-based evaluation relies on. To bridge this gap, we introduce RubricEval. Our benchmark features: (1) the first rubric-level meta-evaluation benchmark for instruction following, (2) diverse instructions and responses spanning multiple categories and model sources, and (3) a substantial set of 3,486 quality-controlled instances, along with Easy/Hard subsets that better differentiates judge performance. Our experiments reveal that rubric-level judging remains far from solved: even GPT-4o, a widely adopted judge in instruction-following benchmarks, achieves only 55.97% on Hard subset. Considering evaluation paradigm, rubric-level evaluation outperforms checklist-level, explicit reasoning improves accuracy, and both together reduce inter-judge variance. Through our established rubric taxonomy, we further identify common failure modes and offer actionable insights for reliable instruction-following evaluation.

AI.tldr: RubricEval is a new benchmark for meta-evaluating how reliably rubrics are used to judge instruction-following in LLMs, showing current rubric-based judges are far from perfect.
AI.motivation: Rubric-based automatic evaluation is widely used to assess instruction-following ability of LLMs, but it is unclear how reliable these rubric-level judgments are. Existing meta-evaluations mostly check only overall response scores, not the fine-grained rubric-item decisions that these evaluations depend on. A dedicated, rubric-level meta-evaluation benchmark is needed to understand and improve these evaluators.
AI.method: The authors build RubricEval, a rubric-level meta-evaluation benchmark containing 3,486 carefully quality-controlled instances of instructions and model responses drawn from diverse categories and model sources. They define a taxonomy of rubrics, construct both Easy and Hard subsets to differentiate judge performance, and systematically test different evaluation paradigms (rubric-level vs checklist-level, with vs without explicit reasoning) using LLMs such as GPT‑4o as judges.
AI.result: Experiments using RubricEval show that rubric-level judging remains challenging: even GPT‑4o achieves only 55.97% accuracy on the Hard subset. Rubric-level evaluation is more accurate than checklist-style scoring, explicit reasoning steps further improve accuracy, and combining rubric-level evaluation with explicit reasoning reduces variance between different judging models. The taxonomy and benchmark highlight systematic failure modes of current rubric-based judges.
AI.conclusion: RubricEval demonstrates that current rubric-based LLM judges are not yet reliably accurate at the fine-grained rubric level that instruction-following benchmarks assume. Rubric-level evaluation with explicit reasoning is preferable to simpler checklist approaches, but significant room for improvement remains. The benchmark, rubric taxonomy, and identified failure patterns provide a foundation and practical guidance for developing more trustworthy instruction-following evaluation methods.

title: Beyond Detection: Rethinking Education in the Age of AI-writing
summary: As generative AI tools like ChatGPT enter classrooms, workplaces and everyday thinking, writing is at risk of becoming a formality -- outsourced, automated and stripped of its cognitive value. But writing is not just output; it is how we learn to think. This paper explores what is lost when we let machines write for us, drawing on cognitive psychology, educational theory and real classroom practices. We argue that the process of writing -- messy, slow, often frustrating -- is where a human deep learning happens. The paper also explores the current possibilities of AI-text detection, how educators can adapt through smarter pedagogy rather than bans, and why the ability to recognize machine-generated language may become a critical literacy of the 21st century. In a world where writing can be faked, learning can not.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that outsourcing writing to generative AI undermines writing’s role as a core cognitive process for learning and thinking.
AI.motivation: Generative AI systems like ChatGPT are increasingly used to produce written text in education, work, and daily life. This trend risks reducing writing to a superficial product, ignoring its deeper role in shaping thought and learning. The authors are motivated to examine what is lost when humans allow machines to take over the act of writing, especially in learning contexts.
AI.method: The paper conceptually synthesizes insights from cognitive psychology, educational theory, and observations from real classroom practices. It likely uses theoretical analysis and illustrative examples rather than empirical experiments to explore how writing supports cognition, and how AI-assisted writing changes that process. It also surveys or analyzes current approaches to AI-text detection and pedagogical responses.
AI.result: The paper finds that writing is a fundamentally cognitive and learning-intensive process; when AI generates text for us, we lose key opportunities for deep processing, reflection, and knowledge construction. It shows that current AI-text detection is limited and imperfect, and that blanket bans on AI tools are pedagogically weak. Instead, it highlights that educators can redesign tasks and assessment to re-center genuine cognitive work, even in an AI-saturated environment.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that writing must be preserved and reimagined as a central human learning activity in the age of generative AI. Educators should not rely on prohibiting AI or on unreliable detection tools; rather, they should develop smarter pedagogies that require authentic engagement with ideas and processes that AI cannot easily fake. Recognizing and critically interpreting machine-generated language will become an essential literacy skill. Ultimately, while AI can fake written products, it cannot substitute for the human learning that occurs through the act of writing.

title: UniAI-GraphRAG: Synergizing Ontology-Guided Extraction, Multi-Dimensional Clustering, and Dual-Channel Fusion for Robust Multi-Hop Reasoning
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems face significant challenges in complex reasoning, multi-hop queries, and domain-specific QA. While existing GraphRAG frameworks have made progress in structural knowledge organization, they still have limitations in cross-industry adaptability, community report integrity, and retrieval performance. This paper proposes UniAI-GraphRAG, an enhanced framework built upon open-source GraphRAG. The framework introduces three core innovations: (1) Ontology-Guided Knowledge Extraction that uses predefined Schema to guide LLMs in accurately identifying domain-specific entities and relations; (2) Multi-Dimensional Community Clustering Strategy that improves community completeness through alignment completion, attribute-based clustering, and multi-hop relationship clustering; (3) Dual-Channel Graph Retrieval Fusion that balances QA accuracy and performance through hybrid graph and community retrieval. Evaluation results on MultiHopRAG benchmark show that UniAI-GraphRAG outperforms mainstream open source solutions (e.g.LightRAG) in comprehensive F1 scores, particularly in inference and temporal queries. The code is available at https://github.com/UnicomAI/wanwu/tree/main/rag/rag_open_source/rag_core/graph.

AI.tldr: UniAI-GraphRAG is an improved GraphRAG framework that uses an ontology-guided schema, richer clustering, and dual retrieval channels to boost RAG performance on complex, multi-hop, and domain-specific questions.
AI.motivation: RAG systems still struggle with complex reasoning, multi-hop questions, and specialized domain QA. Existing GraphRAG approaches help by structuring knowledge into graphs, but they suffer from limited adaptability across industries, incomplete community-level representations, and suboptimal retrieval performance. The paper aims to address these gaps so that graph-based RAG can handle harder reasoning tasks and transfer better to different domains.
AI.method: The authors build UniAI-GraphRAG on an open-source GraphRAG baseline and add three main components: (1) Ontology-Guided Knowledge Extraction: a predefined schema (ontology) steers LLMs to extract domain-specific entities and relations with higher precision and consistency; (2) Multi-Dimensional Community Clustering: they improve graph community quality via alignment completion (filling missing links), attribute-based clustering (grouping by node properties), and multi-hop relationship clustering (capturing longer-range dependencies); (3) Dual-Channel Graph Retrieval Fusion: they combine fine-grained graph retrieval with higher-level community retrieval to balance answer accuracy, reasoning capability, and efficiency in the final QA pipeline.
AI.result: On the MultiHopRAG benchmark, UniAI-GraphRAG achieves higher comprehensive F1 scores than mainstream open-source graph-based RAG systems such as LightRAG, with especially strong improvements on inference-heavy and time-sensitive (temporal) queries. This suggests better multi-hop reasoning and handling of temporal information.
AI.conclusion: The proposed UniAI-GraphRAG framework, with ontology-guided extraction, multi-dimensional community clustering, and dual-channel retrieval fusion, improves the reasoning and retrieval capabilities of GraphRAG systems. Its superior performance on MultiHopRAG, notably for inference and temporal queries, indicates it is a more robust and adaptable solution for complex, domain-specific RAG applications, and the released code supports reproducibility and further extension.

title: Adaptive Chunking: Optimizing Chunking-Method Selection for RAG
comment: Accepted at LREC 2026. 10 pages, 4 figures. Code: https://github.com/ekimetrics/adaptive-chunking
summary: The effectiveness of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is highly dependent on how documents are chunked, that is, segmented into smaller units for indexing and retrieval. Yet, commonly used "one-size-fits-all" approaches often fail to capture the nuanced structure and semantics of diverse texts. Despite its central role, chunking lacks a dedicated evaluation framework, making it difficult to assess and compare strategies independently of downstream performance. We challenge this paradigm by introducing Adaptive Chunking, a framework that selects the most suitable chunking strategy for each document based on a set of five novel intrinsic, document-based metrics: References Completeness (RC), Intrachunk Cohesion (ICC), Document Contextual Coherence (DCC), Block Integrity (BI), and Size Compliance (SC), which directly assess chunking quality across key dimensions. To support this framework, we also introduce two new chunkers, an LLM-regex splitter and a split-then-merge recursive splitter, alongside targeted post-processing techniques. On a diverse corpus spanning legal, technical, and social science domains, our metric-guided adaptive method significantly improves downstream RAG performance. Without changing models or prompts, our framework increases RAG outcomes, raising answers correctness to 72% (from 62-64%) and increasing the number of successfully answered questions by over 30% (65 vs. 49). These results demonstrate that adaptive, document-aware chunking, guided by a complementary suite of intrinsic metrics, offers a practical and effective path to more robust RAG systems. Code available at https://github.com/ekimetrics/adaptive-chunking.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an adaptive, metric-driven framework to select the best chunking strategy per document for RAG, significantly improving answer correctness and coverage without changing models or prompts.
AI.motivation: RAG performance heavily depends on how documents are chunked, but current fixed, heuristic chunking methods ignore document structure and semantics, and there is no intrinsic evaluation framework to compare chunking strategies independent of downstream tasks.
AI.method: They design an Adaptive Chunking framework that, for each document, chooses among multiple chunking strategies using five new intrinsic quality metrics—References Completeness, Intrachunk Cohesion, Document Contextual Coherence, Block Integrity, and Size Compliance. They also introduce two new chunkers (an LLM-regex splitter and a split-then-merge recursive splitter) plus post-processing, and evaluate on a multi-domain corpus.
AI.result: The metric-guided adaptive method improves RAG answer correctness to 72% (from 62–64%) and increases successfully answered questions by over 30% (65 vs. 49) across legal, technical, and social science documents, without modifying models or prompts.
AI.conclusion: Document-aware, adaptively selected chunking guided by complementary intrinsic metrics is an effective, practical way to substantially enhance RAG robustness and performance, and the authors provide code to facilitate adoption.

title: Large Language Model as Token Compressor and Decompressor
summary: In this paper, we establish the novel insight that an off-the-shelf LLM can function as an excellent token compressor and decompressor. To demonstrate, we design a self-expressive autoencoding learning framework fine-tunes a pretrained LLM to translate long texts into a compact internal language of discrete, variable-length latent codes, termed Z-tokens, and to reconstruct the original text exactly from them. The resulting representation is content-adaptive: semantically dense segments receive more Z-tokens, while redundant or predictable regions are aggressively compressed, via lightweight LoRA-based adapter heads. Empirically, our method achieves up to 18 times token reduction on Wikipedia, CNN/DailyMail, HotpotQA, and Qulac-style long-query datasets, while preserving reconstruction fidelity and downstream performance. This simple yet effective design supports applications including prompt compression and autoregressive generation directly in the Z-token space, offering a potential pathway toward token-efficient long-context reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that a standard LLM can be fine-tuned to act as a discrete autoencoder, compressing long texts into short internal codes (Z-tokens) and perfectly reconstructing them, achieving up to 18× token reduction without hurting performance.
AI.motivation: Long-context processing with LLMs is expensive because inference cost scales with token length. There is a need for token-efficient representations that preserve information and performance while reducing sequence length. Existing methods often rely on external compressors or lose fidelity. The authors want a simple way to leverage off-the-shelf LLMs themselves as powerful compressors that maintain exact reconstruction and support downstream tasks.
AI.method: They design a self-expressive autoencoding framework that fine-tunes a pretrained LLM with lightweight LoRA adapter heads. The model learns an internal, discrete, variable-length latent code sequence (Z-tokens) into which long texts are translated. Semantically important or dense parts receive more Z-tokens, while redundant or predictable regions are compressed more. The same (or paired) LLM is trained to decode from Z-tokens back to the original text exactly, enabling lossless compression and decompression. Training is done on corpora like Wikipedia and QA/summary datasets.
AI.result: On datasets including Wikipedia, CNN/DailyMail, HotpotQA, and Qulac-style long queries, the method yields up to 18× reduction in token count while preserving exact or near-exact reconstruction and maintaining downstream task performance. The learned Z-token representation is content-adaptive, allocating code capacity based on semantic density.
AI.conclusion: An off-the-shelf LLM, with minimal LoRA-based modifications and an autoencoding training scheme, can serve as an effective discrete token compressor and decompressor. The approach enables strong token reduction, supports prompt compression and generation directly in the compressed Z-token space, and points toward more token-efficient long-context reasoning with LLMs.

title: The Competence Shadow: Theory and Bounds of AI Assistance in Safety Engineering
summary:
As AI assistants become integrated into safety engineering workflows for Physical AI systems, a critical question emerges: does AI assistance improve safety analysis quality, or introduce systematic blind spots that surface only through post-deployment incidents? This paper develops a formal framework for AI assistance in safety analysis. We first establish why safety engineering resists benchmark-driven evaluation: safety competence is irreducibly multidimensional, constrained by context-dependent correctness, inherent incompleteness, and legitimate expert disagreement. We formalize this through a five-dimensional competence framework capturing domain knowledge, standards expertise, operational experience, contextual understanding, and judgment.
  We introduce the competence shadow: the systematic narrowing of human reasoning induced by AI-generated safety analysis. The shadow is not what the AI presents, but what it prevents from being considered. We formalize four canonical human-AI collaboration structures and derive closed-form performance bounds, demonstrating that the competence shadow compounds multiplicatively to produce degradation far exceeding naive additive estimates.
  The central finding is that AI assistance in safety engineering is a collaboration design problem, not a software procurement decision. The same tool degrades or improves analysis quality depending entirely on how it is used. We derive non-degradation conditions for shadow-resistant workflows and call for a shift from tool qualification toward workflow qualification for trustworthy Physical AI.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a formal framework to analyze how AI assistants affect the quality of safety engineering for Physical AI systems, showing that poorly designed human-AI collaboration can systematically degrade safety analysis via a “competence shadow,” and arguing that we must qualify workflows rather than just tools.
AI.motivation: AI assistants are being integrated into safety engineering workflows for Physical AI systems, but it is unclear whether they actually improve safety analyses or instead create subtle, systematic blind spots that only appear after deployment. Traditional benchmark-based evaluation is ill-suited here because safety competence is multidimensional, context-dependent, incomplete, and subject to reasonable expert disagreement. The authors aim to provide a principled way to reason about AI assistance in this complex setting.
AI.method: The authors construct a formal competence framework with five dimensions: domain knowledge, standards expertise, operational experience, contextual understanding, and judgment. They then define the concept of a “competence shadow” to capture how AI-generated analyses can narrow human reasoning by shaping what is not considered. Using formal modeling, they specify four canonical patterns of human-AI collaboration in safety analysis and derive closed-form performance bounds for each, quantifying how competence shadows propagate and interact in these workflows.
AI.result: The analysis shows that the competence shadow compounds multiplicatively across human-AI interactions, leading to potential degradation in safety analysis quality that is much worse than simple additive intuition would suggest. Depending on how AI assistance is integrated into workflows, the same AI tool can either improve or significantly degrade safety outcomes. The formal results characterize conditions under which collaboration designs exacerbate or mitigate competence shadows.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the key challenge in using AI for safety engineering is not merely selecting or qualifying tools, but designing and governing human-AI workflows. It identifies “non-degradation conditions” under which AI assistance does not worsen safety analysis and advocates shifting regulatory and assurance focus from tool qualification to workflow qualification for trustworthy Physical AI systems.

title: TAPO: Translation Augmented Policy Optimization for Multilingual Mathematical Reasoning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable proficiency in English mathematical reasoning, yet a significant performance disparity persists in multilingual contexts, largely attributed to deficiencies in language understanding. To bridge this gap, we introduce Translation-Augmented Policy Optimization (TAPO), a novel reinforcement learning framework built upon GRPO. TAPO enforces an explicit alignment strategy where the model leverages English as a pivot and follows an understand-then-reason paradigm. Crucially, we employ a step-level relative advantage mechanism that decouples understanding from reasoning, allowing the integration of translation quality rewards without introducing optimization conflicts. Extensive experiments reveal that TAPO effectively synergizes language understanding with reasoning capabilities and is compatible with various models. It outperforms baseline methods in both multilingual mathematical reasoning and translation tasks, while generalizing well to unseen languages and out-of-domain tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Translation-Augmented Policy Optimization (TAPO), a reinforcement learning framework that uses English as a pivot language and separates language understanding from reasoning to improve multilingual mathematical reasoning and translation performance of LLMs.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs are strong at English mathematical reasoning, they perform worse in other languages due to weaker language understanding. Existing methods do not adequately address this gap or the interaction between translation quality and reasoning optimization, leading to suboptimal multilingual performance.
AI.method: The authors introduce TAPO, built on top of GRPO, which enforces an explicit understand-then-reason workflow by using English as a pivot language. TAPO uses a step-level relative advantage mechanism to decouple the optimization of language understanding (via translation quality rewards) from reasoning, so that translation rewards can be incorporated without causing conflicts with reasoning rewards.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple models show that TAPO improves both multilingual mathematical reasoning accuracy and translation quality compared with baseline methods. It also shows good generalization to unseen languages and out-of-domain tasks, indicating that the alignment of understanding and reasoning transfers beyond the training setup.
AI.conclusion: TAPO successfully synergizes language understanding and reasoning in LLMs by explicitly structuring the learning process around translation into English followed by reasoning. This design mitigates the multilingual performance gap, is broadly compatible with different models, and enhances both reasoning and translation, with benefits extending to new languages and tasks.

title: Probabilistic Abstract Interpretation on Neural Networks via Grids Approximation
summary: Probabilistic abstract interpretation is a theory used to extract particular properties of a computer program when it is infeasible to test every single inputs. In this paper we apply the theory on neural networks for the same purpose: to analyse density distribution flow of all possible inputs of a neural network when a network has uncountably many or countable but infinitely many inputs. We show how this theoretical framework works in neural networks and then discuss different abstract domains and corresponding Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverses together with abstract transformers used in the framework. We also present experimental examples to show how this framework helps to analyse real world problems.

AI.tldr: The paper extends probabilistic abstract interpretation to analyze neural networks, enabling the study of how input probability densities flow through networks with infinitely many inputs.
AI.motivation: Testing all possible inputs to a neural network is infeasible, especially when the input space is infinite or continuous. The authors want a principled way to reason about the distribution of all possible inputs and their transformations through the network, rather than just pointwise predictions.
AI.method: They instantiate the framework of probabilistic abstract interpretation for neural networks. This involves defining suitable abstract domains to represent probability distributions over inputs, specifying corresponding Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverses, and designing abstract transformers for layers or components of the network that approximate how densities propagate through the model.
AI.result: They demonstrate how the framework can be systematically applied to neural networks and detail several choices of abstract domains and their associated pseudo-inverses and transformers. They provide experimental examples that show the framework can analyze density flow for real-world neural-network problems.
AI.conclusion: Probabilistic abstract interpretation can be effectively adapted to neural networks to analyze how probability densities over an infinite input space propagate through the model, providing a sound, general framework with practical applicability as shown by experiments.

title: Navigating the Prompt Space: Improving LLM Classification of Social Science Texts Through Prompt Engineering
summary: Recent developments in text classification using Large Language Models (LLMs) in the social sciences suggest that costs can be cut significantly, while performance can sometimes rival existing computational methods. However, with a wide variance in performance in current tests, we move to the question of how to maximize performance. In this paper, we focus on prompt context as a possible avenue for increasing accuracy by systematically varying three aspects of prompt engineering: label descriptions, instructional nudges, and few shot examples. Across two different examples, our tests illustrate that a minimal increase in prompt context yields the highest increase in performance, while further increases in context only tend to yield marginal performance increases thereafter. Alarmingly, increasing prompt context sometimes decreases accuracy. Furthermore, our tests suggest substantial heterogeneity across models, tasks, and batch size, underlining the need for individual validation of each LLM coding task rather than reliance on general rules.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how different types and amounts of prompt context affect the accuracy of LLM-based text classification in social science applications, finding that small, well-designed additions help most, too much context can hurt, and effects vary widely by model and task.
AI.motivation: While LLMs can make text classification in social sciences cheaper and sometimes as accurate as traditional methods, their performance is highly variable. Practitioners lack clear, evidence-based guidance on how to design prompts to get the best possible coding accuracy. The authors aim to understand whether and how different aspects of prompt context—such as label descriptions, extra instructions, and examples—systematically improve or worsen classification results.
AI.method: The authors run controlled experiments on two different social-science text-classification tasks, systematically varying three elements of prompt context: (1) label descriptions, (2) instructional nudges, and (3) few-shot examples. They compare accuracy across prompts with minimal versus more extensive context, and analyze how effects differ across multiple LLMs, tasks, and batch sizes.
AI.result: They find that adding a small amount of carefully chosen prompt context produces the largest gains in accuracy over a bare-bones prompt. Adding more and more context after that typically leads to only small incremental improvements and, in some cases, actually reduces accuracy. They also find large heterogeneity: different models, tasks, and batch sizes respond differently to the same prompt changes.
AI.conclusion: Optimal prompt design for LLM-based text classification is not simply “more context is better.” Instead, modest, targeted additions to the prompt tend to yield the biggest accuracy improvements, and overloading the prompt can even backfire. Because effects vary substantially by model, task, and batching choices, researchers should empirically validate prompt setups for each specific coding application rather than relying on generic prompt-engineering rules.

title: Distribution and Clusters Approximations as Abstract Domains in Probabilistic Abstract Interpretation to Neural Network Analysis
summary: The probabilistic abstract interpretation framework of neural network analysis analyzes a neural network by analyzing its density distribution flow of all possible inputs. The grids approximation is one of abstract domains the framework uses which abstracts concrete space into grids. In this paper, we introduce two novel approximation methods: distribution approximation and clusters approximation. We show how these two methods work in theory with corresponding abstract transformers with help of illustrations of some simple examples.

AI.tldr: The paper extends a probabilistic abstract interpretation framework for neural network analysis by introducing two new grid-based approximation methods—distribution and clusters approximation—with corresponding abstract transformers and examples.
AI.motivation: Existing probabilistic abstract interpretation of neural networks uses grids to approximate the density distribution of all possible inputs but may be limited in precision or scalability. There is a need for improved approximation methods that capture distributional properties or structural groupings (clusters) more effectively while remaining analyzable within the framework.
AI.method: The authors work within a probabilistic abstract interpretation framework that tracks how input probability densities flow through a neural network. They refine the grids abstract domain by proposing two new approximation schemes: (1) distribution approximation, which likely enriches each grid cell with more detailed distributional information, and (2) clusters approximation, which groups regions or states into clusters. For each method, they define corresponding abstract transformers—formal operators that describe how these abstract elements propagate through network layers—and explain them using simple illustrative examples.
AI.result: The paper formally defines the two new approximation methods and their abstract transformers within the probabilistic abstract interpretation framework, and demonstrates through simple examples how they operate on neural network density flows.
AI.conclusion: Distribution approximation and clusters approximation can be integrated into the existing grids-based probabilistic abstract interpretation framework, providing new ways to approximate input density flows through neural networks. These methods are theoretically sound within the framework and are illustrated on simple examples, suggesting they may improve flexibility or expressiveness of neural network analysis.

title: Translation Asymmetry in LLMs as a Data Augmentation Factor: A Case Study for 6 Romansh Language Varieties
summary: Recent strategies for low-resource machine translation rely on LLMs to generate synthetic data from higher-resource languages. We find that this method fails for Romansh, because LLMs tend to confuse its 6 distinct language varieties. Our experiments show that instead, the direction of data augmentation should be aligned with the resource gradient between source and target language. This approach surpasses Gemini 3 Pro in the lowest-resource variety of Romansh by 23 BLEU. A human evaluation confirms that our experiments yield the first model that generates fluent translations in the individual Romansh varieties.

AI.tldr: The paper studies why current LLM-based data augmentation for low-resource machine translation fails for Romansh and proposes resource-aligned augmentation that significantly improves translation quality for its individual varieties.
AI.motivation: Low-resource machine translation often uses large language models to generate synthetic data from higher-resource languages, but this strategy assumes the LLM can properly handle the low-resource language. Romansh has six distinct varieties that LLMs frequently confuse, leading to poor quality synthetic data and degraded translation performance. The paper is motivated by the need to understand this failure mode and to develop a data augmentation strategy that better fits languages with internal variety and strong resource imbalances.
AI.method: The authors analyze LLM behavior on Romansh and identify confusion among its six varieties when generating synthetic training data. They then propose a new augmentation strategy that orients the direction of data generation according to the resource gradient: from relatively higher-resource to lower-resource varieties or language directions, instead of the default higher-resource external language to low-resource Romansh. They train MT models using this resource-aligned synthetic data and compare them to systems trained using standard LLM augmentation and to strong LLM baselines such as Gemini 3 Pro. Both automatic metrics (BLEU) and human evaluations are used.
AI.result: The standard approach of generating synthetic Romansh data from a higher-resource external language with general LLMs fails due to variety confusion. In contrast, the proposed resource-aligned augmentation strategy yields large gains, especially for the lowest-resource Romansh variety, achieving a 23 BLEU improvement over Gemini 3 Pro. Human evaluation shows that the resulting models are the first to produce consistently fluent, variety-specific Romansh translations.
AI.conclusion: For languages with multiple closely related varieties and very low resources, naive LLM-based data augmentation from external high-resource languages can be counterproductive because LLMs mix varieties. Aligning the direction of data augmentation with the internal resource gradient between varieties and languages is crucial. This strategy substantially improves MT quality and enables, for the first time, fluent, variety-faithful translations in all individual Romansh varieties, outperforming a strong general-purpose LLM.

title: A Gait Foundation Model Predicts Multi-System Health Phenotypes from 3D Skeletal Motion
summary: Gait is increasingly recognized as a vital sign, yet current approaches treat it as a symptom of specific pathologies rather than a systemic biomarker. We developed a gait foundation model for 3D skeletal motion from 3,414 deeply phenotyped adults, recorded via a depth camera during five motor tasks. Learned embeddings outperformed engineered features, predicting age (Pearson r = 0.69), BMI (r = 0.90), and visceral adipose tissue area (r = 0.82). Embeddings significantly predicted 1,980 of 3,210 phenotypic targets; after adjustment for age, BMI, VAT, and height, gait provided independent gains in all 18 body systems in males and 17 of 18 in females, and improved prediction of clinical diagnoses and medication use. Anatomical ablation revealed that legs dominated metabolic and frailty predictions while torso encoded sleep and lifestyle phenotypes. These findings establish gait as an independent multi-system biosignal, motivating translation to consumer-grade video and its integration as a scalable, passive vital sign.

AI.tldr: The paper builds a large-scale gait foundation model from 3D skeletal motion that shows gait is a powerful, independent biomarker predicting a wide range of phenotypes across body systems.
AI.motivation: Although gait is known to reflect health, most existing methods use hand-crafted features and focus on specific diseases rather than understanding gait as a general, systemic biomarker. There is a need for a scalable, data-driven representation of gait that captures whole-body health information and can be applied broadly across many traits and clinical outcomes.
AI.method: The authors collected 3D skeletal motion using a depth camera from 3,414 adults performing five motor tasks. They trained a foundation model to learn embeddings (latent representations) of gait from these 3D motion sequences, and compared these learned embeddings to traditional engineered features. They then evaluated how well these embeddings predict many phenotypes (3,210 traits), clinical diagnoses, and medication use, and conducted anatomical ablation experiments to understand which body segments contribute to predicting different phenotypes.
AI.result: The learned gait embeddings outperformed engineered features, predicting age (r=0.69), BMI (r=0.90), and visceral adipose tissue (VAT) area (r=0.82) with high accuracy. Overall, gait embeddings significantly predicted 1,980 out of 3,210 phenotypic targets. After adjusting for age, BMI, VAT, and height, gait still provided independent predictive value across all 18 body systems in males and 17 of 18 in females, and improved prediction of clinical diagnoses and medication use. Anatomical ablation showed legs primarily contributed to predicting metabolic and frailty phenotypes, while the torso captured information related to sleep and lifestyle.
AI.conclusion: Gait, captured as 3D skeletal motion and modeled via a foundation model, functions as an independent, multi-system biosignal that encodes diverse aspects of human health beyond basic anthropometrics. The work supports treating gait as a scalable, passive vital sign and motivates extending the approach to consumer-grade video for broader, real-world health monitoring applications.

title: An Experimental Comparison of the Most Popular Approaches to Fake News Detection
summary: In recent years, fake news detection has received increasing attention in public debate and scientific research. Despite advances in detection techniques, the production and spread of false information have become more sophisticated, driven by Large Language Models (LLMs) and the amplification power of social media. We present a critical assessment of 12 representative fake news detection approaches, spanning traditional machine learning, deep learning, transformers, and specialized cross-domain architectures. We evaluate these methods on 10 publicly available datasets differing in genre, source, topic, and labeling rationale. We address text-only English fake news detection as a binary classification task by harmonizing labels into "Real" and "Fake" to ensure a consistent evaluation protocol. We acknowledge that label semantics vary across datasets and that harmonization inevitably removes such semantic nuances. Each dataset is treated as a distinct domain. We conduct in-domain, multi-domain and cross-domain experiments to simulate real-world scenarios involving domain shift and out-of-distribution data. Fine-tuned models perform well in-domain but struggle to generalize. Cross-domain architectures can reduce this gap but are data-hungry, while LLMs offer a promising alternative through zero- and few-shot learning. Given inherent dataset confounds and possible pre-training exposure, results should be interpreted as robustness evaluations within this English, text-only protocol.

AI.tldr: The paper critically evaluates 12 fake news detection models across 10 English text-only datasets, focusing on robustness and cross-domain generalization under domain shift.
AI.motivation: Although fake news detection models have improved, real-world misinformation is increasingly sophisticated due to LLMs and social media. Existing work often evaluates methods on single datasets, neglecting domain shift, label inconsistencies, and robustness, which limits understanding of how well models work in realistic, cross-domain settings.
AI.method: The authors select 12 representative approaches (traditional ML, deep learning, transformers, and cross-domain architectures) and 10 public fake news datasets. They harmonize heterogeneous labels into a binary Real/Fake scheme, treat each dataset as a separate domain, and conduct in-domain, multi-domain, and cross-domain experiments. They also investigate LLMs in zero- and few-shot setups while explicitly acknowledging confounds such as label semantics and possible pre-training exposure.
AI.result: Fine-tuned models achieve strong performance within individual datasets (in-domain), but their performance drops substantially when tested on unseen domains. Cross-domain architectures alleviate some of this degradation but require large amounts of data. LLM-based approaches, even without extensive fine-tuning, show competitive robustness through zero- and few-shot learning. However, confounding factors in datasets and potential pre-training overlap prevent definitive claims of real-world effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: The study shows that current fake news detection systems are fragile under domain shift, and high in-domain accuracy does not translate to robust generalization. Cross-domain methods and LLMs are promising but face data and evaluation challenges. The paper argues that fake news detection research should prioritize robustness-focused benchmarks, clearer label semantics, and careful consideration of pre-training exposure when assessing the reliability of detection models in practical settings.

title: SliderQuant: Accurate Post-Training Quantization for LLMs
comment: This work is accepted to ICLR 2026. Code is available at https://github.com/deep-optimization/SliderQuant
summary: In this paper, we address post-training quantization (PTQ) for large language models (LLMs) from an overlooked perspective: given a pre-trained high-precision LLM, the predominant sequential quantization framework treats different layers equally, but this may be not optimal in challenging bit-width settings. We empirically study the quantization impact of different layers on model accuracy, and observe that: (1) shallow/deep layers are usually more sensitive to quantization than intermediate layers; (2) among shallow/deep layers, the most sensitive one is the first/last layer, which exhibits significantly larger quantization error than others. These empirical observations imply that the quantization design for different layers of LLMs is required on multiple levels instead of a single level shared to all layers. Motivated by this, we propose a new PTQ framework termed Sliding-layer Quantization (SliderQuant) that relies on a simple adaptive sliding quantization concept facilitated by few learnable parameters. The base component of SliderQuant is called inter-layer sliding quantization, which incorporates three types of novel sliding window designs tailored for addressing the varying quantization sensitivity of shallow, intermediate and deep layers. The other component is called intra-layer sliding quantization that leverages an incremental strategy to quantize each window. As a result, SliderQuant has a strong ability to reduce quantization errors across layers. Extensive experiments on basic language generation, zero-shot commonsense reasoning and challenging math and code tasks with various LLMs, including Llama/Llama2/Llama3/Qwen2.5 model families, DeepSeek-R1 distilled models and large MoE models, show that our method outperforms existing PTQ methods (including the latest PTQ methods using rotation transformations) for both weight-only quantization and weight-activation quantization.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SliderQuant, a post-training quantization framework for large language models that adapts quantization across and within layers using sliding windows, significantly improving low-bit performance over prior PTQ methods.
AI.motivation: Existing post-training quantization methods for large language models typically quantize layers sequentially with a uniform scheme, implicitly assuming all layers have similar sensitivity to quantization. However, in low bit-width regimes, this assumption may be suboptimal and can cause large performance degradation. The authors empirically find that shallow and deep layers, especially the very first and last layers, are much more sensitive to quantization than intermediate layers and incur larger quantization errors. This motivates the need for a multi-level, layer-aware quantization strategy that tailors quantization strength and configuration to the varying sensitivity of different layers to preserve accuracy while maintaining high compression.
AI.method: The authors introduce SliderQuant, a PTQ framework built on an adaptive sliding quantization concept with a small number of learnable parameters. It has two main components: (1) Inter-layer sliding quantization: they design three types of sliding windows across the depth of the model to treat shallow, intermediate, and deep layers differently, adapting quantization granularity and configuration to each region’s sensitivity. The windows slide over the layer stack so that neighboring layers share or smoothly transition between quantization settings instead of using a single global scheme. (2) Intra-layer sliding quantization: within each layer (or window), they apply an incremental quantization strategy, quantizing parts of the parameters step by step so that quantization errors are controlled and their accumulation is mitigated. Together, these mechanisms allow the quantization parameters to adapt both across layers and within layers while remaining lightweight and compatible with standard PTQ workflows.
AI.result: Through extensive experiments on multiple evaluation suites—basic language generation, zero-shot commonsense reasoning, and more challenging math and code tasks—the authors show that SliderQuant yields consistently lower quantization error and higher task accuracy than prior PTQ approaches. The evaluation spans diverse LLM families, including Llama, Llama2, Llama3, Qwen2.5, DeepSeek-R1–distilled models, and large mixture-of-experts models, under both weight-only and joint weight-activation quantization. SliderQuant outperforms strong baselines, including recent PTQ methods that employ rotation transformations, particularly in more aggressive low-bit settings where sensitivity differences between layers matter most.
AI.conclusion: Layer sensitivity to quantization in large language models is heterogeneous, with the first and last layers being especially fragile. A uniform per-layer PTQ strategy is thus suboptimal, especially for low bit-width configurations. By introducing sliding, window-based quantization across and within layers, SliderQuant provides a simple yet effective way to adapt quantization strength and configuration to layer-wise sensitivity. This leads to significantly reduced quantization error and better preservation of model performance across a wide range of architectures and tasks, establishing SliderQuant as a strong, practical PTQ solution for modern LLMs.

title: Humans vs Vision-Language Models: A Unified Measure of Narrative Coherence
comment: 9 pages of content, 1 page of appendices, 9 tables, 3 figures
summary: We study narrative coherence in visually grounded stories by comparing human-written narratives with those generated by vision-language models (VLMs) on the Visual Writing Prompts corpus. Using a set of metrics that capture different aspects of narrative coherence, including coreference, discourse relation types, topic continuity, character persistence, and multimodal character grounding, we compute a narrative coherence score. We find that VLMs show broadly similar coherence profiles that differ systematically from those of humans. In addition, differences for individual measures are often subtle, but they become clearer when considered jointly. Overall, our results indicate that, despite human-like surface fluency, model narratives exhibit systematic differences from those of humans in how they organise discourse across a visually grounded story. Our code is available at https://github.com/GU-CLASP/coherence-driven-humans.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how coherent stories generated by vision-language models are compared to human-written stories on a visually grounded storytelling dataset, revealing systematic discourse-level differences despite fluent text.
AI.motivation: Although modern vision-language models produce fluent stories from images, it is unclear whether their narratives are organized as coherently as human stories at a deeper discourse level. Existing evaluations focus mostly on surface quality or single aspects of coherence rather than a comprehensive, structured assessment. The authors aim to fill this gap by systematically characterizing and quantifying narrative coherence in visually grounded narratives from both humans and models.
AI.method: The authors use the Visual Writing Prompts corpus, which pairs images with human-written stories, and generate corresponding stories with several vision-language models. They design a suite of coherence-related metrics capturing coreference behavior, discourse relation usage, topic continuity, character persistence over the narrative, and multimodal character grounding to the images. These metrics are combined into an overall narrative coherence score, and human vs. model narratives are compared in terms of individual metrics and their joint profile.
AI.result: Vision-language models exhibit broadly similar coherence patterns to each other, but these patterns systematically differ from those of human writers across the coherence metrics. While individual metric differences can be subtle, the combined metric profile reveals clearer and more consistent distinctions between human and model narratives. The analysis shows that fluent model texts still diverge from human discourse organization in visually grounded storytelling.
AI.conclusion: Narratives produced by current vision-language models, though linguistically fluent, lack human-like organization at the discourse level when telling stories about images. A multifaceted metric-based analysis exposes systematic coherence differences between human and machine narratives that may be missed by surface-level evaluation, highlighting the need for future work on models and training methods that better capture human-like narrative structuring in multimodal contexts.

title: DAGverse: Building Document-Grounded Semantic DAGs from Scientific Papers
summary: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) are widely used to represent structured knowledge in scientific and technical domains. However, datasets for real-world DAGs remain scarce because constructing them typically requires expert interpretation of domain documents. We study Doc2SemDAG construction: recovering a preferred semantic DAG from a document together with the cited evidence and context that explain it. This problem is challenging because a document may admit multiple plausible abstractions, the intended structure is often implicit, and the supporting evidence is scattered across prose, equations, captions, and figures. To address these challenges, we leverage scientific papers containing explicit DAG figures as a natural source of supervision. In this setting, the DAG figure provides the DAG structure, while the accompanying text provides context and explanation. We introduce DAGverse, a framework for constructing document-grounded semantic DAGs from online scientific papers. Its core component, DAGverse-Pipeline, is a semi-automatic system designed to produce high-precision semantic DAG examples through figure classification, graph reconstruction, semantic grounding, and validation. As a case study, we test the framework for causal DAGs and release DAGverse-1, a dataset of 108 expert-validated semantic DAGs with graph-level, node-level, and edge-level evidence. Experiments show that DAGverse-Pipeline outperforms existing Vision-Language Models on DAG classification and annotation. DAGverse provides a foundation for document-grounded DAG benchmarks and opens new directions for studying structured reasoning grounded in real-world evidence.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DAGverse, a framework and dataset for automatically constructing semantic directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) from scientific documents that contain DAG figures, providing high-precision, document-grounded DAGs for benchmarking and studying structured reasoning.
AI.motivation: Real-world semantic DAG datasets are scarce because building them usually demands labor-intensive expert annotation across complex scientific documents. Existing work struggles because documents can support multiple plausible graph abstractions, the intended structure is often implicit, and the supporting evidence is scattered across text, equations, captions, and figures. There is a need for scalable, high-precision methods and benchmarks that link DAG structures directly to their evidential grounding in documents.
AI.method: The authors exploit scientific papers that already contain explicit DAG figures as weak supervision. They propose DAGverse, whose main component DAGverse-Pipeline is a semi-automatic pipeline that: (1) classifies figures to identify DAGs, (2) reconstructs the underlying graph structure from those figures, (3) semantically grounds graph nodes and edges to concepts and relations evidenced in the surrounding document (prose, equations, captions, figures), and (4) validates these mappings, including expert review for quality. As a case study, they apply this pipeline to causal DAGs to build a curated dataset.
AI.result: Using DAGverse-Pipeline, the authors construct DAGverse-1, a dataset of 108 expert-validated semantic DAGs where each graph, node, and edge is linked to supporting evidence in the source document. Empirical evaluations show that DAGverse-Pipeline surpasses existing vision-language models on tasks like DAG figure classification and semantic annotation, demonstrating both higher precision and more reliable grounding in the text.
AI.conclusion: DAGverse demonstrates that leveraging papers with explicit DAG figures enables scalable, high-quality construction of document-grounded semantic DAG datasets. The resulting DAGverse-1 corpus and pipeline outperform current vision-language baselines and provide a foundation for new benchmarks and research on structured reasoning and semantic graph recovery grounded in real scientific evidence.

title: PICon: A Multi-Turn Interrogation Framework for Evaluating Persona Agent Consistency
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based persona agents are rapidly being adopted as scalable proxies for human participants across diverse domains. Yet there is no systematic method for verifying whether a persona agent's responses remain free of contradictions and factual inaccuracies throughout an interaction. A principle from interrogation methodology offers a lens: no matter how elaborate a fabricated identity, systematic interrogation will expose its contradictions. We apply this principle to propose PICon, an evaluation framework that probes persona agents through logically chained multi-turn questioning. PICon evaluates consistency along three core dimensions: internal consistency (freedom from self-contradiction), external consistency (alignment with real-world facts), and retest consistency (stability under repetition).
