The authors argue that benchmarks like WildASR, together with the provided analytical tools, are necessary to guide safer, more reliable deployment of ASR systems in production voice agents and similar applications.

title: LogSigma at SemEval-2026 Task 3: Uncertainty-Weighted Multitask Learning for Dimensional Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis
summary: This paper describes LogSigma, our system for SemEval-2026 Task 3: Dimensional Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (DimABSA). Unlike traditional Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA), which predicts discrete sentiment labels, DimABSA requires predicting continuous Valence and Arousal (VA) scores on a 1-9 scale. A central challenge is that Valence and Arousal differ in prediction difficulty across languages and domains. We address this using learned homoscedastic uncertainty, where the model learns task-specific log-variance parameters to automatically balance each regression objective during training. Combined with language-specific encoders and multi-seed ensembling, LogSigma achieves 1st place on five datasets across both tracks. The learned variance weights vary substantially across languages due to differing Valence-Arousal difficulty profiles-from 0.66x for German to 2.18x for English-demonstrating that optimal task balancing is language-dependent and cannot be determined a priori.

AI.tldr: LogSigma is a Dimensional Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis system that uses learned homoscedastic uncertainty, language-specific encoders, and ensembling to optimally balance Valence and Arousal regression, achieving top performance on multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: Traditional Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis predicts discrete sentiment labels, but SemEval-2026 Task 3 (DimABSA) requires continuous Valence and Arousal scores, which differ in prediction difficulty across languages and domains. This asymmetry makes it hard to manually tune loss weights for multi-task regression, motivating an automatic, data-driven way to balance these objectives.
AI.method: The paper proposes LogSigma, a multi-task regression framework with learned homoscedastic uncertainty, where each regression target (Valence and Arousal) has a learned log-variance parameter that weights its loss term. The system uses language-specific encoders to better capture cross-lingual differences, and multi-seed ensembling to stabilize and improve predictions. The learned log-variance terms dynamically adjust the contribution of each task during training, based on their inherent difficulty in each language/domain.
AI.result: LogSigma attains first place on five datasets across both tracks of the SemEval-2026 DimABSA task. Analysis of the learned variance weights shows large cross-lingual differences in the relative difficulty of Valence vs. Arousal prediction, with weights ranging from 0.66x for German to 2.18x for English, indicating substantial variation in optimal task balancing across languages.
AI.conclusion: Automatically learning task-specific uncertainty-based weights is effective for balancing Valence and Arousal regression in DimABSA, especially in multilingual settings where task difficulty profiles vary. The strong leaderboard results and the diversity of learned weights suggest that language-dependent optimization is necessary and that manual, fixed balancing strategies are suboptimal for this problem.

title: Imperative Interference: Social Register Shapes Instruction Topology in Large Language Models
summary: System prompt instructions that cooperate in English compete in Spanish, with the same semantic content, but opposite interaction topology. We present instruction-level ablation experiments across four languages and four models showing that this topology inversion is mediated by social register: the imperative mood carries different obligatory force across speech communities, and models trained on multilingual data have learned these conventions. Declarative rewriting of a single instruction block reduces cross-linguistic variance by 81% (p = 0.029, permutation test). Rewriting three of eleven imperative blocks shifts Spanish instruction topology from competitive to cooperative, with spillover effects on unrewritten blocks. These findings suggest that models process instructions as social acts, not technical specifications: "NEVER do X" is an exercise of authority whose force is language-dependent, while "X: disabled" is a factual description that transfers across languages. If register mediates instruction-following at inference time, it plausibly does so during training. We state this as a testable prediction: constitutional AI principles authored in imperative mood may create language-dependent alignment. Corpus: 22 hand-authored probes against a production system prompt decomposed into 56 blocks.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that changing the grammatical register (imperative vs declarative) of system prompts can flip an AI model’s behavior from competitive to cooperative in some languages, especially Spanish, even when the semantic content is the same.
AI.motivation: Existing work assumes that prompts are interpreted by models mainly as technical specifications. This paper questions that assumption by investigating whether models instead treat prompts as social actions, which might differ across languages and cultures. The authors want to understand why the same instruction can lead to different behaviors in different languages and whether grammatical mood and social register are responsible.
AI.method: The authors design 22 hand-authored probe prompts based on a real production system prompt, breaking it into 56 instruction blocks. They create versions of these prompts in four languages and manipulate the grammatical form (imperative vs declarative) of specific blocks. Using four different models, they run instruction-level ablation experiments and measure how responses change, focusing on whether the interaction topology is cooperative or competitive. They also statistically evaluate cross-linguistic variance and test how rewriting some imperative blocks into declaratives changes behavior.
AI.result: They find that Spanish and English can yield opposite interaction topologies (competitive vs cooperative) even with semantically equivalent prompts, driven by differences in how the imperative mood functions socially. Rewriting just one imperative instruction block into a declarative form reduces cross-linguistic variance in behavior by 81% with statistical significance (p = 0.029). Rewriting three of eleven imperative blocks is enough to shift Spanish behavior from competitive to cooperative, and this change also affects blocks that were not rewritten, suggesting a global shift in model behavior.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that language models interpret system instructions as social acts whose force depends on language-specific norms, especially the social meaning of the imperative mood. Imperative formulations (e.g., “NEVER do X”) function as exercises of authority with language-dependent strength, whereas declarative formulations (e.g., “X: disabled”) behave more like language-agnostic factual settings. Because these effects appear at inference, the authors hypothesize that similar register effects occur during training, implying that alignment methods using imperative-style constitutions may induce language-dependent alignment outcomes. They propose this as a testable prediction for future work.
